Tafseer:
'[All] praise is [due] to Allah, who has sent down upon His servant the Book.' This Surah begins with the same theme with which the previous Surah, Bani Israel, concluded (see verses 105 to 111). This is an expression of the right of that great blessing which was revealed to the Arabs in the form of the Qur'an—showing that this blessing must motivate gratitude, not disbelief. Unfortunate are those who undervalue it and search for new excuses to deny it.

The Way of the Qur'an: 'and has not made therein any deviance. [He has made it] straight.' This is the attribute of this Book—that God has kept no crookedness or complexity in it, neither in terms of expression nor in terms of meaning. Its language is clear Arabic, and its guidance is toward that straight path, the proofs of whose straightness are present in every corner of intellect, human nature, and the cosmos, and which the Qur'an has explained in so many different ways that no one who uses his intellect can fail to understand them. Only those will remain deprived of them who do not use their intellect. This very attribute of the Qur'an has also been stated in Surah Bani Israel: 'Indeed, this Quran guides to that which is most suitable.'

The Purpose of the Qur'an: 'to warn of severe punishment from Him and to give good tidings to the believers.' This describes the purpose of revealing this Book—that it is a warning for those who deny, and a glad tiding for those who believe. In the first part, 'the disbelievers' or 'the deniers' is omitted, because 'the believers' is present in the subsequent part, which makes the omitted term clear on the principle of contrast. The subject of the verb 'yundhira' (to warn) can be Allah or 'His servant', i.e., the Messenger. Under the first possibility, the meaning is: Allah has revealed this Book specifically from Himself to warn the disbelievers of a severe punishment. Under the second possibility, the meaning is: the Messenger of Allah warns people of the severe punishment revealed by God. In the first case, the words 'from Him' (min ladunhu) indicate special care—showing that God Himself made arrangements for this purpose under His watch. In the second case, it expresses the intensity of the punishment—showing that this punishment will be no ordinary punishment, but will be the wrath of God from which no one will be able to protect.

'in which they will remain forever.' They will remain in this 'good reward' forever, meaning they will remain in that Paradise which will be obtained as the fruit and result of this 'good reward'. Using a term to refer to its ultimate result is a well-known style of Arabic eloquence, examples of which have passed in this book before and will come ahead too.

'And to warn those who say, "Allah has taken a son."' This is a specific mention after a general one. That is, specifically to warn those who assume offspring for God, worship them, and become completely indifferent to God, relying on their intercession. This refers to both the polytheists of Arabia and the Christians. The polytheists of Arabia called the angels daughters of God, and the Christians made Jesus the son of God.

The Blind Following of the Polytheists: 'They have no knowledge of it, nor had their fathers. Grave is the word that comes out of their mouths; they speak not except a lie.' 'Knowledge' (ilm) here means 'proof'. That is, those who have assumed sons and daughters for God, associating them as partners with Him, have fabricated this story from their own minds. Neither has God said this anywhere, nor do these foolish ones possess any proof for it. Those forefathers whom they follow blindly in this foolishness also possessed no proof for it; they chose this misguidance purely out of ignorance, and these people are following in their footsteps out of ignorance too. He stated: grave indeed is the word that comes out of their mouths. This is a fabrication and slander against Allah, and a challenge to His jealousy. The meaning is: if they do not desist from their insolence, they will face their fate very soon.

Affectionate Consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): 'Then perhaps you would kill yourself through grief over their footsteps, if they do not believe in this message.' This offers consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and the style of consolation is very affectionate—showing that you are melting away in grief over their lack of faith, to the extent that it appears you will destroy yourself if they do not believe in this Book. This shows how intense was the Prophet's feeling of responsibility regarding his messengership. You spent your nights and days in the work of calling; yet, you were consumed by the grief that if people were not believing, it might be due to some shortcoming on your part. This feeling increased both your struggle and your grief. Upon this, Allah affectionately checked him, showing that such excess in the feeling of responsibility is not correct. These ungrateful and unappreciative people who are not believing in this Book—do not assume that they face any difficulty in understanding it, or that there is any shortcoming in your conveying the message; rather, the actual cause of it is something else.

The Actual Cause of the Deniers' Denial: 'Indeed, We have made what is on the earth adornment for it that We may test them as to which of them is best in deed.' This uncovers the actual cause of their aversion and denial. He stated that this world is a place of trial (Dar-ul-Imtihan). In it, We are testing who uses his intellect to become a seeker of the Hereafter, and who follows his desires to become a worshipper of this world. To fulfill the requirements of this trial, We have applied a deceptive cosmetic of beauty and adornment over the face of this world. There is great attraction and charm in its wealth, offspring, crops, gardens, cars, mansions, and offices. Its pleasures are ready and immediate, and its bitterness is hidden. Contrary to this, all the successes of the Hereafter are prospective, and to obtain them, its seekers have to suffer countless immediate trials in this very world. This trial is a severe trial; succeeding in it is not the work of every ambitious person. Only those will succeed in it whose insight is so deep that—no matter how much this world tempts them—they recognize this deceptive world in all its guises and never become trapped in its love to sacrifice the eternal reward of the Hereafter, even though every particle of this universe bears witness to its truth.

The End of This World: 'And indeed, We will make that which is upon it [into] a barren ground.' 'Juruz' refers to a barren land devoid of water and vegetation. The meaning is: these foolish people who are infatuated with the things of this earth—a time will come when We will destroy all these things and make this earth like a barren, flat ground. This same theme is coming ahead in verses 46 to 48; if Allah wills, more details will be presented there.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 9 to 26: Questions Instigated by the People of the Book: The manner in which the Quraysh opposed the Qur'an and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was mentioned in the previous verses. An aspect of this opposition was that in this era, to corner the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the People of the Book instigated various complex questions, which the Quraysh took up to demand answers from him. The questions regarding the soul, Dhul-Qarnayn, and other such matters referenced in the Qur'an were raised by the People of the Book. A similar question regarding the Companions of the Cave (Ashab-ul-Kahf) was raised by the People of the Book, and through their instigation, the Quraysh presented it before the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). These irrelevant questions were obviously not asked for the sake of obtaining knowledge and research, but were asked solely to trouble the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). The opponents' purpose was: if you do not answer, they will use this silence to claim: 'Look, he claims prophethood but cannot answer our question'; and if you answer and any point goes against their assumptions, they will use it to claim: 'Look, what absurd things he is speaking; the reality is this, and he says the opposite.'

The Context of the Story of the Companions of the Cave: That the story of the Companions of the Cave has come here in response to the opponents' question is clear from the Qur'an itself. In verses 22 to 24 ahead, it is explicitly stated that the Qur'an has addressed this story in response to the question. Regarding such questions, the attitude directed by the Qur'an is—as will become clear ahead—to avoid getting involved in them and to bypass them. But if any question was useful, or if its answer could be made a medium for useful teaching, the Qur'an answered it as much as was necessary. Since the life of the Companions of the Cave could be highly instructive for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions in this trial-filled era—as you and your companions were passing through the same stages through which the Companions of the Cave had passed—Allah narrated as much of their story as was true. In this way, the question of the objectors was answered, a lesson of patience and determination was given to the Prophet and his companions from a pure story of the past, and a highly important chapter of the history of the people of truth—which was lost in the veil of legends—emerged anew, completely clean of all dust.

Who Were the Companions of the Cave? Who were the Companions of the Cave? To what era of history do they belong, and among whose ancestors are they? Giving any definitive answer to these questions is highly difficult. Definitively, only as much can be said as the Qur'an has described; beyond this, whatever exists is merely legend. By describing the correct and instructive part of their story, the Qur'an has restricted further search and debate regarding them, as will become clear. For this reason, we do not prefer to pursue a matter from which the Qur'an has restricted us. However, from assumptions and indications, it is certain that these people did not belong to the history of the Quraysh or the Jews, but belonged to the history of the Christians. Among them, such accounts are found which bear resemblance to the event of the Companions of the Cave. Our view is that in the early period of their history, when the Christians were persecuted by the Romans for their religion, the Companions of the Cave played that part in the way of the call to truth which the Qur'an has mentioned. These people raised the call to monotheism and the Hereafter with complete fearlessness, to the extent that their entire environment became their enemy, and a danger arose that the opponents would stone them.

When matters reached this limit, these people took refuge in a cave, and Allah provided those means in this cave for their protection which Allah alone can provide. Later, when their conditions became known to people and the signs of divine support manifested for them became famous, memorials were established for them, and every group began to claim relationship with them. In this way, their mention remained as a holy tradition among the Christians, but this mention gradually became limited to a few miracles and useless debates. The actual achievement of their lives became completely hidden from sight. They debated on questions like: what was their number? were they three and the fourth their dog, or five and the sixth their dog? but no one knew what their call was, what sacrifices they made in its path, and these debates probably lasted as long as some remnants of original Christianity existed. Later, when Paul completely distorted Christianity, all the original traditions of their ancestors also became hidden. Among the Christians of Arabia, it appears that some traditions regarding these people existed, but in a completely distorted form. Through the instigation of these Christians, this question was brought before the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and the Qur'an answered it because—as we have stated—it could provide great strength to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions in this trial-filled era. Just as the Companions of the Cave were targets of atrocities in their environment, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions were also targets of the Quraysh's atrocities; and just as the Companions of the Cave had to take refuge in a cave, you and your companions were also to face the stages of migration to Abyssinia, the cave of Thawr, and migration to Madinah. Now read the subsequent verses in this light:

'Or have you thought that the companions of the cave and the inscription were, among Our signs, a wonder?'

Singular Address to a Group: The address 'hasabu' (have you thought) is singular, but it is not necessary that it be directed only to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him); like the address 'Alam tara' (Have you not considered), it can be general. In this style of address, as we have mentioned in more than one place, every individual of the addressed group is meant, and it carries more force than a plural address. In this very context ahead, there are several singular addresses, but they are directed to the listener, not to the Prophet; see: 'wa tarash-shamsa' (and you see the sun) in verse 17, 'lawittala'ta' (if you had looked), 'la-wallayta' (you would have turned), 'wa la-muli'ta' (and you would have been filled).

This Address is to the Objectors: Here, it is also worth considering: what is the occasion to address the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and ask if he considers the Companions of the Cave and the Inscription as something strange and rare among Our signs? The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was originally not aware of this story; he became aware of it first through this revelation, and even if he had been somewhat aware of it, why would he consider it strange and rare? Far more strange stories of the past prophets had been narrated to him. Why then would he consider the matter of the Companions of the Cave as strange and rare? In our view, this question is to the objectors, and the reason is that those who raised this question had the primary purpose of reducing the value of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions in the eyes of the people—implying: 'Among us, such great saints as the Companions of the Cave have passed, for whom the greatest signs of God's power manifested, so why do we need anyone else's guidance, particularly of a prophet in whom we do not see any such thing?' Keep in view that in this era, both the Jews and the Christians had openly begun to say: 'Whoever wants guidance should become a Jew or a Christian, what is this new religion? Even the polytheists' religion is better than this!' Facing this hidden mindset of the objectors, the Qur'an began the reply by stating: if you consider the story of the Companions of the Cave as a highly strange thing, it is no strange thing. This is one sign among the countless signs of God. Similar signs have appeared before, and will appear ahead too for those whom God wills. These signs are in the hand of God; no specific group has a monopoly over them.

Our view regarding the address of the verse is this; but if anyone wants to consider the address as directed to the Prophet, then the interpretation of the verse would be: if you consider the story of the Companions of the Cave as strange based on the well-known traditions, it is no strange thing. Similar signs of God have appeared before, and will appear ahead too. In this, there is a glad tiding for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and his companions—showing that just as God previously showed His power to protect the bearers of His religion, He will show His power for you too in every stage.

The Cause of the Title 'Companions of the Cave and the Inscription': As to why they were called 'Companions of the Cave and the Inscription' (Ashab-ul-Kahf wa-r-Raqeem), the answer is: they were known by this title among the Christians of Arabia. Their relation to 'the cave' (Al-Kahf) is obvious because they took refuge in a cave. As for 'the inscription' (Al-Raqeem), in our view, the correct view is what Ikrimah narrated from Ibn Abbas: Ka'b thought that Al-Raqeem was the name of the town from which the Companions of the Cave emerged to take refuge. Al-Raqeem also means a valley. Researching the origin of names is both unnecessary and highly difficult. Non-Arab names become so changed when they come into Arabic that researching their origin becomes like digging a mountain, and it holds no specific importance for the purpose of instruction. If this name had been given by the Qur'an, then its meaning and origin would have been highly important. But this name, as we have stated, was adopted by the Christians. The Qur'an merely adopted it. According to some modern researchers, Al-Raqeem is the same city which is known as Petra, and the Arabs know it as Batra.

'[Mention] when the youths retreated to the cave and said, "Our Lord, grant us from Yourself mercy and prepare for us from our affair right guidance."'

The Meaning of 'Rushd': 'Rushd' means obtaining guidance and steadfastness. 'Rashida amruhu' means he obtained guidance in his matter. 'And prepare for us from our affair right guidance' means: 'O our Lord, provide for us guidance and steadfastness in this path which we have chosen.'

The Youths' Prayer: This is the prayer those youths made when they decided to take refuge in the cave. It is clear from the words of the verse that they were youths. When the zeal for truth arises in youths, they care neither for compromise nor for dangers. But these people did not possess merely youthful passion; they also possessed the light of wisdom bestowed by Allah. For this reason, in this delicate stage, they prayed for guidance and steadfastness, and this is what is befitting for believers.

The Summary of the Story as an Introduction: Note that this verse and the subsequent two verses serve as a summary of the story, placing the entire story before the reader in brief. After this, the complete story will come in detail. The benefit of presenting the summary before the detail is that the actual purpose remains in view, preventing subsequent details from obscuring it. This method of describing stories has been adopted by the Qur'an in several places.

'So We cast [a cover of sleep] over their ears within the cave for a number of years.'

The Meaning of 'Casting over the ears' (Darb 'ala al-aadhaan): Literally, it means to seal or pat the ears. From this, the idiom became used for stopping someone from hearing or putting them to sleep with affection. The reason is that when putting a child to sleep, his ears are patted. After they took refuge in the cave, Allah put them to a deep, peaceful sleep for several years. These words are used as a metaphor for sleep, and it is a highly beautiful metaphor for putting someone to sleep with affection.

'Then We awakened them that We might show which of the two factions was most calculating of what [duration] they had remained.'

The preposition 'li' (to) in 'li-na'lama' is in the sense of outcome and end, and 'na'lama' (We know) here means 'to see and test'. The meaning of the verse is: after this long sleep, We awakened them so that this matter might end in this result: they should form two groups to dispute over the question of how long they remained asleep—some saying this, others saying that. In this way, We would test them as to whether any group could calculate this duration, and ultimately it would become clear that none of them could calculate it. Moreover, this reality would become clear to them: the same will be the case with the life of the barzakh—no one will have any feeling of its duration, and upon rising, everyone will assume that they had just slept and woken up. This theme is coming ahead in more detail in verse 19; all its hidden aspects will become clear there.

Detail After Summary: 'We relate to you, [O Muhammad], their story in truth. Indeed, they were youths who believed in their Lord, and We increased them in guidance.'

Now, after the summary, their story is being related in detail. The condition 'in truth' (bil-haqq) on one hand expresses the reality that—ignoring those legends which were famous among their namesakes—the Qur'an has presented the actual reality of the event. On the other hand, it shows that the Qur'an is not merely storytelling like others, but the actual purpose is to make people aware of the wisdom and admonition present in this story. He stated that these were some youths who believed in their Lord and protected their faith, in return for which Allah increased them in guidance. How did this increase manifest? Its description is coming in the subsequent verse. Keep in view the word 'youths' (fityah) here. By stating that these people belonged to the class of youths, the Qur'an has drawn the attention of the youths of the time—particularly those who believed in the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—so they may obtain a lesson from this story and rise for the call to truth, unconcerned by any danger, just as they did. God will be their helper and supporter in every stage.

'And We bound [i.e., made firm] their hearts when they stood up and said, "Our Lord is the Lord of the heavens and the earth. Never will we invoke besides Him any deity. We would have certainly spoken, then, an excessive transgression."'

'Rabata-Allahu 'ala qalbihi' means: God gave strength and determination to his heart. 'Shatat' means to be far from truth, i.e., extreme deviation and distance from the truth.

The meaning is: although the conditions were highly severe, and raising the call of truth was a highly dangerous task, God blessed their faith, the effect of which was that they did not remain content with their own faith to sit in their houses, but rose for the call of monotheism, unconcerned by any danger. When they stood up, Allah—according to His sunnah—blessed their hearts with strength and determination, and they made a public declaration among their nation: 'Our Lord is only He Who is the Lord of the heavens and the earth. We are not ready to accept any other deity besides Him. If we worship any other besides Him, our action would be highly improper and far from the truth.'

Challenging the Nation: 'These, our people, have taken besides Him deities. Why do they not bring for [worship of] them a clear authority? And who is more unjust than one who invents about Allah a lie?'

'People' (qawm) here refers to the people of Al-Raqeem. This is a challenge from these truth-loving youths to their entire nation, and those who possess a taste for Arabic grammar know that the word 'ha'ulaa' (these) is used here in a manner that expresses contempt. The third-person pronouns also carry contempt. The meaning is: these people made a public declaration: 'These fools of our nation who have set up other deities besides Allah—why do they not bring some clear proof in support of their deities? After all, who can be more unjust than one who invents a lie against God?!'

'[The youths said to one another], "And when you have withdrawn from them and that which they worship other than Allah, retreat to the cave. Your Lord will spread out for you of His mercy and will prepare for you from your affair facility."'

'Mirfaq' refers to something of necessity and benefit. 'And will prepare for you from your affair facility' means: in this stage, whatever you are in need of, Allah will provide it for you.

Glad Tiding of Support from Allah: This is a glad tiding for them from Allah, and the context indicates that a part of the speech is omitted here which is clear on its own—meaning: ultimately, the conflict between them and their nation increased so much that a danger arose that the people would stone them, as clear from verse 20. At that time, these people decided to take refuge in a specific cave which they had selected beforehand. In this stage, Allah blessed them through His revelation with the glad tiding: now when you have left your nation and its deities for the sake of Allah, take refuge in your selected cave; your Lord will spread His mercy for you and arrange for all your needs. Allah wants His servants only to rise for His path with determination; when they rise, He Himself provides the provisions for the subsequent journey. 'And whoever fears Allah - He will make for him a way out, And He will provide for him from where he does not expect.'

Removal of a Doubt: A question might arise in someone's mind: were these people recipients of revelation that Allah blessed them through His revelation? The answer is: this revelation was of the same type as the revelation through which Moses's mother was directed to place her child in a chest and cast it into the river.

Wrong Argument of Sufi Thinkers in Support of Abandoning the World: From this event, some Sufi thinkers have attempted to prove the virtue of isolation and abandoning the world. But this is not correct in our view. These people took refuge in this cave only when they had played their lives for the reform of their society and reached the stage of being stoned by their nation. The stage is exactly the same stage that was faced by our Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), and he had to take refuge in the cave of Thawr. These people did not choose this cave-dwelling for monasticism, but chose it to save their lives from the evil of the enemies of truth.

Misunderstanding of the Commentators: Generally, our commentators have not taken this verse as a glad tiding from Allah, but have considered it as a statement of the Companions of the Cave themselves—claiming that they said this to one another. But this is not correct. With the definiteness with which the promise of Allah's help and support is made in the verse, no humble servant can speak in this manner. If a humble servant speaks such a thing, he will speak only in words of hope, expectation, and prayer, as their prayer is referenced in verse 10.

'And [had you been present], you would see the sun when it rose, inclining away from their cave on the right, and when it set, passing by them on the left, while they were in a broad open area thereof. That is from the signs of Allah. He whom Allah guides is the [rightly] guided, but he whom He leaves astray - never will you find for him a protecting guide.'

'Tazawaru' is originally 'tatazawaru'. It means to incline away, deflect, and turn aside. 'Taqriduhum'—'qard' means to cut and deflect. From this, the idiom 'qarda-l-makan' arose, meaning: he deflected from that place. 'Fajwah' refers to a space, gap, or corner between two things; from this, it became used for the courtyard of a house.

Divine Provision of Life's Needs in the Cave: This describes those facilities (marafis) which Allah promised to provide for them in the cave—showing that if you had been present, you would see that when the sun rose, it rose deflecting to the right of their cave, and when it set, it set deflecting to their left, while they were resting in the courtyard of this cave. From this, we learn that the mouth of the cave was situated in such a way that air, light, and warmth—which are among the necessities of life—entered it easily, but the heat of the sun did not find a way inside. Our commentators have attempted to determine the direction and facing of the cave and its mouth, but this is unnecessary in our view. Various forms can be assumed, but nothing can be said with certainty about any of them. The correct thing is what Allah has stated—that this is a sign among the signs of Allah: He, through His strategy and care, provided such a cave for His servants where all their needs of life were provided without any effort, and it appeared as if even the sun passed by them with respect and reverence—conveying its service but causing no disturbance to their rest and comfort.

A Warning: 'That is from the signs of Allah. He whom Allah...'-—in connection with this sign of Allah, this warning has been given: as far as the signs of Allah are concerned, signs greater than one another are present. But only those obtain guidance from these signs whom Allah blesses with the enablement. He who is deprived of this enablement, no one else can become his protector and guide.

'And you would think them awake, while they were asleep. And We turned them to the right and to the left, while their dog stretched his forelegs at the entrance. If you had looked at them, you would have turned from them in flight and been filled with terror.'

Arrangement for Protection: This describes the arrangement Allah made for their protection—showing that although they were sleeping in the cave, the situation was such that if anyone happened to see them, he would not assume they were sleeping, but would think they were awake. That is, no one, assuming them to be sleeping and helpless, could dare to harm them; rather, he would think they were alert and watching over themselves. The posture of their sleep was such that they appeared to have merely lain down for a moment to rest. It was not such that a viewer would think they were fast asleep. 'And We turned them to the right and to the left.' He stated: We also kept turning them from side to side. This turning is necessary to maintain physical health, and also to prevent the body from becoming stiff by lying in one posture for a long time. Since these people slept on the earth for a long duration, if they had lain on one side for a long time, the earth might have harmed their bodies. For this reason, just as an affectionate mother keeps changing the sides of her child in the cradle, the hand of power kept changing their sides. 'while their dog stretched his forelegs at the entrance'—this is also part of the arrangement of protection: their faithful dog remained seated at the entrance of the cave with his forelegs stretched out, as if keeping a watch. Clearly, this dog also slept throughout this duration, but the posture of his sleep was not that of ordinary dogs, but was that of a guard dog keeping a watch at the entrance. Allah made this arrangement so that no person or wild animal would dare to enter the cave. 'If you had looked at them, you would have turned from them in flight and been filled with terror.'—obviously, if anyone happened to see such a sight, he would be terrified and run away. He would be left astonished: who are these people lying in this manner within a cave in the midst of mountains? Whether he considered them good people or bad, thieves and dacoits or monks and ascetics, heavenly creatures or earthly, humans or jinn and angels, under all circumstances, an unknown fear would surely overcome him. This atmosphere of fear was created there by the hand of power for the protection of His servants. Worldly rulers sleep in palaces secured with barbed wires, guarded by armed soldiers, yet their lives are not secure. The servants of Allah sleep in caves and forests, and no bird has the courage to touch them.

'And similarly, We awakened them that they might question one another. A speaker among them said, "How long have you remained [here]?" They said, "We have remained a day or part of a day." They said, "Your Lord is most knowing of how long you have remained. So send one of you with this silver coin of yours to the city and let him look to which is the purest of food and bring you provision from it and let him be cautious and not let anyone be aware of you."'

Another Sign of Care: 'And similarly' (wa-kazaalika)—meaning, just as We showed that sign of Our care in their matter which has been mentioned above, similarly We showed this sign too: We awakened them from this sleep so that it might end in this result: they should ask one another how long they remained asleep, and ultimately it would become clear to them that they are completely unable to calculate this duration—only Allah knows it. Consequently, one of them asked his companions: 'How long do you think you have spent in this state?' His companions replied: 'At most a day or even less.' Ultimately, they agreed that the knowledge of this duration belongs to Allah alone, so discussing this question is of no use. 'But do this: send one of you with this money to the city; let him first find out in which part of the city pure food is available, bring some food from there, and let him go cautiously, letting no one know about you.'

Dispute Among the Companions of the Cave Regarding the Duration of Their Sleep: This is the very dispute which is referenced briefly in verse 12. Here, its details are given—showing that this dispute occurred among themselves. Here too, the preposition 'li' (to) in 'li-yatasaa'aloo' is in the sense of outcome and end—showing that Allah wanted their awakening to end in this result: they should ask one another about the duration of their sleep, and this reality would become clear to them: they are completely unable to calculate this duration; and from this, this reality would also become clear to them: the same will be the case with the life of the barzakh. When people rise on the Day of Resurrection, they will feel as if they had remained in that state for a day or even less. 'let him look to which is the purest of food'—meaning, in which part or side of the city. 'Pureset' (azka) refers to pure food.

Emphasis on Inquiring with Caution: When they took refuge in the cave, due to the dominance of disbelief and polytheism in their nation, there was no distinction between the prohibited and the permitted; for this reason, these people placed specific emphasis on the messenger making careful inquiry as to in which part of the city pure food was likely to be available. After all, there must have been some People of the Book in Al-Raqeem, so it was expected that they would maintain the distinction between the prohibited and the permitted. But in this inquiry, there was also a danger of being discovered. For this reason, they also placed this condition: maintain complete caution, letting no one know. 'Talat_tuf' means to do a thing with caution, skill, and secrecy. The word 'wariq' used in the verse is generally translated by people as coin or rupee, but we have translated it as money, because 'wariq' means silver, and this in itself can stand for a coin; for this reason, we have chosen a word which can apply to both minted and unminted forms.

'"Indeed, if they come to know of you, they will stone you or return you to their religion, and never will you succeed, then, ever."

This expresses the fear because of which caution and secrecy were emphasized. In the era when these people took refuge in the cave, persecution of the people of truth had reached its limit, and these people had sought this refuge out of fear of being stoned. Keeping this fear in view, they said: if people come to know of us, there is no safety for us. Previously we escaped somehow, but now if they find us, they will either stone us or force us back into their religion. Then, we will never find salvation from them.'

'And similarly, We caused them to be discovered that they [i.e., the people] might know that the promise of Allah is true and that of the Hour there is no doubt. [That was] when they disputed among themselves over their affair and [some] said, "Construct over them a structure. Their Lord is most knowing of them." Those who prevailed in their matter said, "We will surely take [i.e., build] over them a masjid."'

This 'similarly' (wa-kazaalika) is conjoined to the previous 'similarly'—meaning, just as We showed that sign, similarly We showed this sign too: We made people aware of them. There is no explicit mention here as to how people became aware of them. It is possible that when the companion went to the market to buy food, some clever people recognized him and pursued him to find his path, and in this way, people gradually reached the cave. Pondering over this indicates that when the Companions of the Cave realized that people had obtained some news of them, they remained inside their cave, and in this very state, Allah caused them to die. People became fully aware of them only after their death.

Testimony in the Event of Resurrection: 'that they might know that the promise of Allah is true and that of the Hour there is no doubt.' That is, Allah made people aware of their state so that this might stand as a sign that the promise of Resurrection is true and its occurrence is beyond doubt. Regarding the Resurrection, the greatest doubt the deniers face is that they consider resurrection after death as highly impossible. The event of the Companions of the Cave presented a fresh testimony to remove this impossibility—showing that after sleeping for a long duration, they stood up again by their Lord's command. Indications show that just as Allah made people aware of their cave, He also made them aware of how their lives were spent in the cave. Although there is no explicit mention of this in the verse, it is clear that people knew this, and indeed this was the thing because of which their lives became a wonder in the eyes of people, and after death, they became the center of devotion. It is possible that when people tried to find their path, this secret also opened, and later its report attained the status of a continuous tradition.

'when they disputed among themselves over their affair...'—now, the Qur'an has pointed to that change of condition which occurred after their death: there was a day when these poor ones were hiding in a cave out of fear of being stoned, and now a day came when people began to dispute among themselves to associate themselves with them. Obviously, this dispute occurred among various groups, sects, and families—each wishing to associate them with their own group, sect, and belief. This world has always dealt with the people of truth in this manner: in life, they had to suffer the pelting of stones, but after death, their statues were worshipped. With these people too, after death, different groups and sects rose claiming closeness to them. One group suggested: 'Leave the matter of their religion and belief to Allah, but let us build a monument over their cave.' But the other group, which held the majority, said: 'We will build a masjid over them,' and this group's view prevailed. This gives some idea of the change of condition which occurred as a result of these brave ones' sacrifices—showing that those who were monotheists and worshippers of God became the majority in the land. Others who were not monotheists also experienced such change and tolerance that they too wished to obtain the honor of establishing a monument for the Companions of the Cave.

Building a Masjid Over Them: In our view, a prefix is omitted in 'Their Lord is most knowing of them'—meaning: 'most knowing of their religion'. It appears that this group suggested that the debate over their religion and belief should not be raised, but as the mutually agreed elders and guides of the nation, a monument should be built over their cave; but the other group did not accept this proposal, deciding to build a masjid, and this view prevailed. By calling their monument a masjid, the Qur'an has clarified that these people were monotheists and worshippers of God. For this reason, they built a house of worship for God. Remember that the houses of worship of the Jews and Christians were originally masjids; corruption arose in them only when these nations adopted polytheistic beliefs and polluted their houses of worship with polytheism. Recall that the Qur'an has counted the 'monasteries' (sawami') and 'churches' (biya') of the Jews and Christians under the category of masjids; see Surah Al-Hajj.

'They will say there were three, the fourth of them being their dog; and they will say there were five, the sixth of them being their dog - guessing at the unseen; and they will say there were seven, and the eighth of them was their dog. Say, "My Lord is most knowing of their number. None knows them except a few." So do not argue about them except with an obvious argument and do not inquire about them among [the speculators] from anyone.'

Instruction to Avoid Useless Questions: Now, addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) as a parenthetical statement, He stated: the actual reality of the Companions of the Cave is what has been related to you; but do not assume that these objectors will become satisfied with it and obtain lessons from their lives; rather, now some people will say: 'Their number was three, and the fourth was their dog'; and some will say: 'They were five, and the sixth was their dog.' He stated that all these statements are 'guessing at the unseen' (rajman bil-gheybi)—merely talking in the air, holding no reality. Furthermore, some will say: 'They were seven, and the eighth was their dog.' Tell them: 'My Lord alone knows best their number. None knows them except a few.' 'So do not argue about them except with an obvious argument.'—meaning, if people bring such questions before you and want to argue with you, do not get involved with them, but speak to them briefly and bypass them. Now, there is no need to inquire about the Companions of the Cave from anyone; when Allah has described their actual story, who is more knowing than Allah that one should refer to him? This verse clarifies that those who are infected with the disease of debate have no interest in the actual reality. If you close one way of debate for them, they will open another; for this reason, it is not befitting for a wise person to try to convince such people. It is best to escape from them. Instead of arguing with them seriously, one should try to bypass them gracefully.

Some interpreters have considered the last statement regarding their number as closer to the truth. The reason is that while the Qur'an declared the first two statements as 'guessing at the unseen', He did not state any such thing regarding the third statement. This inference is correct, but our statement regarding their number is what the Qur'an has stated: 'Say, "My Lord is most knowing of their number. None knows them except a few."'

Commentary on Verses 23 and 24: 'And never say of anything, "Indeed, I will do that tomorrow," Except [when adding], "If Allah wills." And remember your Lord when you forget and say, "Perhaps my Lord will guide me to what is nearer than this to right conduct."

Promises Must Be Made Conditional on Allah's Will: In this connection, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was also directed: do not make an unconditional promise regarding anything that you will do this tomorrow. This instruction was probably needed because when the objectors asked about the Companions of the Cave, you might have promised: 'I will give the answer tomorrow.' Such questions, as we have stated, were asked to test you; for this reason, a natural desire arose in you to answer them quickly, and you promised relying on the guidance of revelation. As far as the desire to answer quickly is concerned, due to the intense desire in the Prophet's heart for the success of his call, he wished to answer every question and objection of the opponents immediately so that no excuse remained on their part for not accepting the truth; but there was another aspect to this matter which was overlooked—showing that sometimes the requirement of divine wisdom regarding a question is that it should not be answered, or if answered, should not be answered immediately. For this reason, it is not appropriate for a prophet to make an unconditional promise relying on revelation; rather, he must make every promise conditional on the will of God, and if he ever forgets, he should remember his Lord upon remembering, telling those to whom the promise was made: 'What a wonder that my Lord guide me to the correct thing in a duration even shorter than the promised duration.'

'And they remained in their cave three hundred years and exceeded by nine.'

Connection of Verse 25 with the Words Above: The parenthetical statement which began in verse 22 concluded in verse 24. Now, this verse relates to those very statements which have been quoted above regarding their duration of sleep. That is, just as they will make various statements regarding their number, they will claim regarding their duration of stay in the cave: 'They remained in the cave three hundred years, and exceeded by nine.' Generally, people have taken this as news from Allah, but in our view, the Qur'an has not said any definitive thing regarding their duration of stay. In verse 11, the words are 'for a number of years'. These are indeed proof that these people remained in the cave for several years; but for expressing the duration of three hundred and nine years, those familiar with Arabic grammar know that these words are completely inappropriate. Furthermore, the subsequent verse 'Say, "Allah is most knowing of how long they remained"' is completely contrary to this meaning. If this had been news from Allah that they remained three hundred and nine years, then there was no appropriate occasion to state immediately after it: 'Tell them that Allah alone knows best their duration of stay.'

Let no one have the misunderstanding that I am unique in this interpretation. The view of several other imams of commentary is the same. And the knowledge is with Allah.

'Say, "Allah is most knowing of how long they remained. He has [knowledge of] the unseen of the heavens and the earth. How clearly He sees of it and hears! They have not besides Him any protector, and He shares not His legislation with anyone."'

This is the answer taught to be given: just as their calculations regarding the number of the Companions of the Cave are completely guessing at the unseen, similarly their calculation regarding their duration of stay in the cave is also completely incorrect. Tell them: Allah alone knows best how long they remained. This statement of Allah, as we indicated above, refutes the view of those who take the words 'And they remained in their cave three hundred years...' as news from Allah. If that had been the case, there was no appropriate occasion to state immediately after it: 'Allah alone is the best Knower of their duration of stay.' Note also this grammatical point: if this had been news from Allah, the sentence would not have begun with a conjunction (waw), but would have begun without a conjunction. The conjunction is a clear indicator that this belongs to those baseless statements which have been quoted above from them.

True Knowledge of the Secrets of Heavens and Earth Belongs to Allah Alone: 'He has [knowledge of] the unseen of the heavens and the earth.' That is, the true knowledge of all the secrets of the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah alone. Others possess only as much knowledge as He chose to reveal; the rest is merely a collection of assumptions. It cannot have the status on the basis of which a human should mock the knowledge revealed by Allah. Today, man has gathered a wealth of information by exploring the depths of the earth, and its usefulness cannot be denied. But it holds no weight against the realities described by the Qur'an.

Allah's All-Encompassing Knowledge: 'How clearly He sees of it and hears!' This is a style of exaggeration and extreme praise—showing that how clearly indeed He sees and hears! No word or thing in the heavens and the earth is outside His all-encompassing hearing and sight. The meaning is: when this All-Knowing, All-Aware Being has given the answer to a question, who is more knowing than Him that one should refer to him? 'They have not besides Him any protector, and He shares not His legislation with anyone.' When the discussion has reached Allah's all-encompassing knowledge, from here the way is cleared to negate all His assumed partners—showing that if people have assumed some protectors and helpers against the One Who is the best Knower of everything, they will not avail them at all. Allah is perfect and independent of all in His essence. He does not associate anyone in His authority.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 27 to 31: The subsequent subject is connected with the subject of the introduction of the Surah. Recall that in the introduction of the Surah, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was directed not to exhaust himself over those who, in their love for the luxury of the world, have forgotten God and the Hereafter and are not ready to hear the words of the Qur'an. After this, in response to a question, the Companions of the Cave were mentioned—showing that Allah does not let His servants who rise for His path go to waste, but comforts them, encourages them, and helps them from the unseen in the difficulties they face in the path of truth. Now, the same introductory subject is taken up again. Addressing the Prophet, He directed: recite what is revealed to you of the Book of your Lord, and care nothing for the opponents' new demands and their aversion and arrogance. The law of Allah regarding guidance and misguidance cannot change. Pay no attention to these wealthy and arrogant ones, and focus all your attention on your poor companions, telling these arrogant ones: 'The truth has come from your Lord, so whoever wills - let him believe; and whoever wills - let him disbelieve.' Whoever disbelieves will face his end himself. Read the subsequent verses in this light:

'And recite, [O Muhammad], what has been revealed to you of the Book of your Lord. There is no changer of His words, and never will you find in other than Him a refuge.'

This is an instruction to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): keep reciting what is revealed to you of the Book. Neither care for the opponents' new demands, nor be anxious over their questions, nor grieve over their stubbornness and denial. The law Allah has established for guidance and misguidance is definitive; no one can change God's laws and regulations. Only those will receive guidance who prove worthy of receiving the enablement, and the enablement is granted only to those who prove their capability for this blessing. This matter is entirely in the hand of Allah, and there is no master or refuge besides God.

'And keep yourself patient [by remaining] with those who call upon their Lord in the morning and the afternoon, desiring His countenance. And let not your eyes pass beyond them, desiring adornment of the worldly life, and do not obey one whose heart We have made heedless of Our remembrance and who followed his desire and whose affair is ever in excess.'

'Furutan' means extravagance, oppression, and exceeding limits. 'Al-amru al-furutu' refers to an affair which has completely exceeded limits.

Instruction to the Prophet Regarding the Arrogant: The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) desired the faith of the leaders and wealthy of his nation not, God forbid, for his own self, but for the sake of elevating the religion. You assumed that their coming to faith would open the path of faith for others. Under this assumption, you sometimes became so occupied with them that you overlooked the rights of your devoted poor companions. In this verse, you were restricted from this. It was stated: make those the center of your attention who call upon their Lord morning and evening, desiring His pleasure. Pay no attention to these seekers of the world who follow their desires and whose affair has now exceeded all limits. Your call carries its own provisions, honor, and majesty, and is not in need of their wealth and resources that you should extend your eyes toward them. Those familiar with the tone of the language know that the intensity of this address is directed not towards the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), but towards the arrogant and wealthy who looked down upon the poor companions and demanded that the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) remove them before they would agree to hear his words.

'And say, "The truth is from your Lord, so whoever wills - let him believe; and whoever wills - let him disbelieve." Indeed, We have prepared for the wrongdoers a fire whose walls will surround them. And if they call for relief, they will be relieved with water like murky oil, which scalds [their] faces. Wretched is the drink, and evil is the resting place.'

Tell the arrogant: 'This is the truth which has been revealed from God, and which I have conveyed to you. Now, the responsibility for anyone's disbelief or faith does not lie upon me; whoever wills - let him believe; and whoever wills - let him disbelieve.' Those who choose disbelief—for such wrongdoers, a fire is ready whose walls will surround them, and when they cry for water, they will be given water like melted copper which will scald their faces. Wretched indeed is the drink, and wretched is the resting place—both being terrifying in their horror.

'Murtafaqan' means a place of resting and reclining. We have translated it as resting place in view of its wide meaning. 'Shawaa yashwee' means to roast or scald. Using 'muhl' (murky oil/melted copper) for the water is to express its intense heat.

Commentary on Verses 30 and 31: Reward of Those Who Believe: 'Indeed, those who have believed and done righteous deeds - indeed, We will not allow to be lost the reward of any who did well in deed. Those will have gardens of perpetual residence; beneath them rivers will flow. They will be adorned therein with bracelets of gold and will wear green garments of fine silk and heavy brocade, reclining therein upon thrones. Excellent is the reward, and good is the resting place.'

After describing the end of those who disbelieved, this describes the end of those who believed in the Qur'an. Since the leaders and wealthy looked down upon these poor believers due to their poverty, those things are highlighted in the description of their reward which were the source of pride and vanity for the wealthy of the time. It is stated: those who believed and performed righteous deeds, We will not let the reward of these good-doers go to waste. For them will be gardens of eternity, beneath which rivers flow. They will be adorned with bracelets of gold, and will wear green garments of fine silk and heavy brocade, reclining upon thrones. Excellent indeed is the reward, and excellent is the resting place.

We have written regarding the descriptions of Paradise and Hell that they belong to the category of 'mutashabihat' (metaphorical verses). The Qur'an brings the descriptions of that unseen world closer to our minds through those illustrations and comparisons which can bring them closer to our understanding. As for their actual reality, its knowledge belongs to Allah alone. The exact nature of the palaces of Hell, or the bracelets, silk, and brocade of Paradise, cannot be known in this world. Note also that in describing these things, the Qur'an has regard for the information and taste of the Arabs, because in illustrations and comparisons, only those things are effective with which the addressees are familiar.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 32 to 44: An Illustration of the Mindset of the Believer and the Disbeliever: In the following, the mindset of both the believer and the disbeliever is clarified through an illustration of two men—showing how a believer looks at this world and how a disbeliever looks at it. Through this illustration, the actual misunderstanding of those who opposed the Qur'an and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is made completely clear. They, in reality, considered their success in this world as proof of the correctness of their thoughts and actions, and when the Muslims warned them of God and the Hereafter, they said: 'Since our condition in the world is better than yours, our belief and way must surely be better too.' Furthermore, they concluded from this: 'Firstly, the Resurrection and Hereafter is merely a legend; and even if it exists, we will remain better off than you there too.' Read the subsequent verses in this light:

'And present to them an example of two men: We granted to one of them two gardens of grapevines, and We bordered them with coconut palms and placed between them fields of crops.'

An Illustration as a Mirror for the Quraysh: This is an illustration presented to the arrogant of the Quraysh so they may see their own open and hidden states in its mirror, and the end of this conflict between truth and falsehood may also stand before them. This is an illustration of two men: one of whom was given two excellent gardens by God, and the other, though possessing no such wealth, had his breast filled with the wealth of faith and his heart filled with the recognition of God.

The Mention of Two Gardens is for the Completion of Blessings: The mention of two gardens is to express the completion of blessings. In Surah Ar-Rahman too, two gardens are mentioned for the people of Paradise. This is to express the completion of blessings. According to the Arabs, the concept of an excellent garden was: a garden of grapevines, with date palms along its borders which would yield fruit, increase the beauty of the garden, and protect it from the dangers of hot winds. Furthermore, there should also be small agricultural plots within the garden where necessary crops could be cultivated according to the season, and a river should flow through its midst with channels running to every corner.

'Each of the two gardens produced its fruit and did not fall short thereof in anything. And We caused to gush forth within them a river.'

If one is blessed with such gardens, and two instead of one, what else does he need in this world? It is stated: both gardens produced their fruit fully, falling short in nothing. A river flowed through their midst, which was the guarantee of their freshness and fertility.

'And he had fruit, so he said to his companion while he was conversing with him, "I am greater than you in wealth and mightier in [numbers of] men."'

Showing Ingratitude Upon Receiving Blessings: This describes the conduct of the owner of these blessings upon receiving them. Its requirement was that he should remain grateful to the Lord Who bestowed these blessings; but what happened was that when his companion advised him to be grateful and obedient to God, warning him of the consequences of ingratitude and God's wrath, he began to argue: 'Tell me, is your condition better or mine? I have more wealth than you, and my group is also stronger than yours, so what are you threatening me with? What I possess is in itself an irrefutable proof that my belief and action are more correct than yours.' 'And he had fruit' points to the fact that this debate occurred at the time of harvest. Clearly, the intoxication of wealth becomes twofold at the time of harvest.

Commentary on Verses 35 and 36: 'And he entered his garden while he was unjust to himself. He said, "I do not think that this will ever perish. And I do not think the Hour will occur. And even if I should be returned to my Lord, I will surely find better than this as a return."

The Meaning of 'Unjust to his own self' (Zalimun-li-nafsihi): That is, he entered his garden acting arrogantly and proudly, claiming: 'I do not think this garden of mine will ever perish. The Resurrection and Hereafter is not going to occur, but even if it did occur, as this mullah warns, I will surely find a better life than this there too.'

'His companion said to him while he was conversing with him, "Have you disbelieved in He who created you from dust and then from a sperm-drop and then proportioned you as a man?"'

The Admonition of the Believing Servant: This is the objection of the believing servant to the arrogant's pride and denial of the Resurrection—showing that if you deny the Resurrection, this is disbelief. Do you assume that God cannot restore you to life after death? Have you not considered your own creation—how God created your ancestor from dust, then began the creation of the offspring of Adam from a sperm-drop, and from this sperm-drop, He proportioned you as a healthy man? If you see this power of God, why do you consider it impossible for Him to resurrect you?! This is indeed the denial of His power and wisdom, which is disbelief.

'But as for me, He is Allah, my Lord, and I do not associate with my Lord anyone.'

This is the belief expressed by the believing servant, clarifying that for faith, merely accepting that God exists is not sufficient; rather, it is also necessary to accept Him alone as one's Lord, Provider, and Master. If anyone acknowledges Allah but accepts others as lords too, he is a polytheist; and this polytheism is also disbelief. If anyone is proud of his wealth and honor, considering it as the result of his own capability and personal desert, and this illusion is established in his mind that no one can snatch it from him, then this too is polytheism, because such a person considers himself a partner in God's sovereignty. The requirement of monotheism is that whatever blessing a servant receives, he should consider it as God's grace and favor, remaining grateful to his Lord for it, considering himself accountable before God for it, and believing that God can snatch this blessing whenever He wills.

'And why did you, when you entered your garden, not say, "What Allah willed [has occurred]; there is no power except in Allah"? If you should see me less than you in wealth and children...'

The Requirement of Faith: This is the correct requirement of faith taught by the believing servant to the arrogant—showing that if you found me possessing less wealth and children than you, this was no occasion to act arrogantly and proudly; rather, it required you to offer gratitude to your Lord for His favor. Its right was that when you entered your green garden loaded with fruits, you should have said: 'Ma sha'a-Allahu la quwwata illa billah'—that is, acknowledged that all this is a favor of the Merciful Lord, and without Him no one has the power to make or mar anything. 'Ana' (I) here has come merely as a separator between the two objects, holding no direct relation to the sentence. Examples of such additional use exist in Arabic speech and in our own language too.

'It may be that my Lord will give me [something] better than your garden and will send upon it a calamity from the sky, and it will become a barren, slippery plain.'

'Husban' is the plural of 'husbanah', which means a bolt of thunder or hail. 'Zalaq' refers to that ground which is completely barren and devoid of any vegetation. The meaning is: if you have mocked my poverty, it is highly possible that my Lord grant me a garden better than yours, and send a bolt of thunder or hail from the sky upon your garden, and it becomes a barren, flat plain.

'Or its water will become sunken [into the earth], so you would never be able to find for it a search.' 'Ghara-l-ma'u gawran' means the water sank deep into the earth. The meaning is: even if there is no thunder or hail, if this flowing river from which the garden is irrigated sinks deep into the earth, what can you or anyone else do?

The Veil Over the Eyes of the Arrogant: Above, the statement of the arrogant was quoted: 'I do not think that this will ever perish.' Seeing his fresh garden loaded with fruits, he said: 'I do not think this will ever perish.' This is the very mindset of all the arrogant and wealthy of the world. They become so lost in the comfort they enjoy that the concept of any breach appearing in it feels highly difficult to them. For this reason, if anyone tries to wake them from their sleep of heedlessness, they wonder: 'What possibility is there of any crack appearing in our fortress!' But when the time comes, the hand of power demolishes the entire fortress in a single movement, and at that time, everyone sees that what they considered highly distant has appeared right beneath their feet. The believing servant pointed to this very reality.

'And his fruits were ruined [i.e., encompassed], so he began to turn his hands over [in dismay] for what he had spent upon it, while it had collapsed upon its trellises, and said, "Oh, I wish I had not associated with my Lord anyone."

'Uheeta bihi' means it was completely destroyed.

Regret After the Opportunity Has Passed: Ultimately, what happened was what the believing servant had warned of: some earthly or heavenly calamity came and destroyed the entire garden in an instant. The trellises upon which the grapevines were supported fell to the ground, and the entire garden was ruined. Now, the arrogant's eyes opened; he saw that the garden whose destruction he considered impossible was ruined in the blink of an eye. The ruined garden lay before him like a corpse, and he stood over its remains wringing his hands—realizing that far from earning any profit from it, even what he spent on its maintenance went to waste. At that time, he remembered his adviser's words and said with deep regret: 'Oh, I wish I had not associated anyone with my Lord.'

'I had not associated anyone with my Lord'—meaning: 'I wish I had not fallen into this pride and arrogance, assuming that all this is the result of my own capability and personal desert; but had considered it as His favor and remained grateful to Him.'

'And there was for him no company to support him other than Allah, nor was he [himself] able to defend himself.' That is, when the punishment came, neither did that group help him upon which he took pride, claiming: 'I have more wealth and a mightier group than you,' nor did he possess any strength in himself to defend himself.

'There the authority is for Allah, the Truth. He is best in reward and best in outcome.'

Punishing the Wicked is Redress for the Righteous: 'There' (Hunalika) points to the appearance of the hour of punishment. The meaning is: when that hour comes, all authority will belong to the True God alone; no one else's word will avail, and He will reward His righteous servants with the best reward and the best outcome. Since the end of the wicked had been described, only the end of the good-doers is described here. Furthermore, remember that the purpose behind any punishment—whether in this world or the next—is primarily to redress the righteous. Punishing the wicked is not the primary purpose, but is the necessary result of it.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 45 to 49: In the following, the illustration of the life of this world is presented—showing that in its love, these heedless ones were so blinded that they were not ready to hear the words of the Qur'an and the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him). He stated that among all the adornments of this world, nothing is going to accompany man; only his righteous deeds will accompany him, so whoever wants to earn, let him earn righteous deeds. Read the verses:

'And present to them the example of the life of this world: [it is] like rain which We send down from the sky, and the vegetation of the earth mingles with it and [then] it becomes dry remnants, scattered by the winds. And Allah is ever, over all things, Perfect in Ability.'

'The vegetation of the earth mingles with it' means that by virtue of the rain, the vegetation of the earth grows abundantly, and due to its abundance, they become mingled with one another. Here, 'the life of this world' refers to that life which was under discussion in the previous section—showing that its adornments and beauties are what make man forget God and the Hereafter. In Surah Al-Hadeed, this summary is explained: 'Know that the life of this world is but play and amusement and adornment and boasting to one another and competition in increase of wealth and children - like the example of a rain whose [resultant] plant growth pleases the tillers; then it dries and you see it turned yellow; then it becomes [scattered] debris.'

Illustration of the Life of this World: In this verse, there are several things worthy of discussion which we will address in their proper place. Here, it is intended to draw attention only to the reality that the life of this world whose illustration is given is also explained in the verse—showing that it refers to the life of play, boasting, and competition in which the wealthy lose themselves, closing their eyes to all realities. Comfort, movies, theatres, cars, mansions, wealth, bank balances, and high offices—nothing else holds any value in their eyes, nor do they have any opportunity to pay attention to anything else. Similarly, in the verses under discussion, He stated: tell them that this beauty of the world is temporary. The rain has brought a temporary beauty; but that hour is not distant when only dust will be seen flying over it. God is powerful over everything; just as He showed this spring, He can show its autumn too.

'Wealth and children are [but] adornment of the worldly life. But the enduring good deeds are better with your Lord for reward and better for hope.'

Empty Adornments: Since in Arabia, protection and defense depended entirely on family and tribal strength, among the signs of a man's greatness was the abundance of offspring along with wealth. In their gatherings of boasting, they mentioned the abundance of offspring particularly. In the boasting mentioned above too, the words: 'I have more wealth and a mightier group' reflect this mindset.

The meaning of the verse is: wealth and offspring in whose love you are crazy are merely the temporary adornments of this world, and this world and all its adornments are temporary. None of these things possesses eternity and stability. With God, in terms of reward and hope, the better things are those righteous deeds which remain forever. If hope is to be pinned, pin it on them; eternal blessings and results will be obtained through them alone.

'And [warn of] the Day We will remove the mountains and you will see the earth prominent [i.e., bare], and We will gather them and not leave behind from them anyone.'

'And you will see the earth prominent.' That is, today this earth appears loaded; there are mountains erected in it, and grand palaces and mansions standing on it; it is adorned with gardens and fields; but a day will come when—let alone other things—even its mountains will be uprooted, and it will become a bare, flat plain. All its beauty will be snatched from it. He stated: on that Day, We will gather everyone, leaving behind no one—neither rich nor poor, master nor slave, worshipper nor worshipped.

'And they will be presented before your Lord in rows, [and He will say], "You have certainly come to Us just as We created you the first time. But you claimed that We would never make for you an appointment."

Returning Empty-Handed: On that Day, all will be presented before your Lord in rows. Today, they act arrogantly in the pride of their wealth and status; but on that Day, they will stand before their Lord in rows like slaves, and We will tell them: see, just as you came into the world empty-handed, today you have returned to Us empty-handed. That is, neither do you have your servants with you today, nor those means and resources upon which you took pride in the world. 'And you have left what We bestowed upon you behind your backs.' 'But you claimed that We would never make for you an appointment'—meaning: you had no expectation of this helplessness and deprivation; you assumed that no day was appointed for your accountability.

'And the record [of deeds] will be placed [open], and you will see the criminals fearful of that within it, and they will say, "Oh, woe to us! What is this book that leaves not a small thing or a great thing but has enumerated it?" And they will find what they did present [before them]. And your Lord does not injustice to anyone.'

The Record of Deeds: 'The record' (Al-Kitab) refers to the record of people's deeds. He stated: this record will be placed before people. At that time, the criminals—knowing what they did—will be fearful of what is inside. When it is opened and their entire reality stands before them, they will cry out: 'Oh, woe to us! What a book is this that has left no small or great thing without recording it!' and they will find what they did present before them. He stated: this arrangement is because your Lord does not want to do injustice to anyone; He will reward everyone according to what they earned.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 50 to 59: In the following, reference is made to the story of Adam and Iblees to warn the Quraysh: in this denial and rejection of yours, you are following strictly the path of Iblees. You have made this Iblees and his offspring your protectors and deities, whereas he has challenged the offspring of Adam with hostility until the Day of Resurrection. This is indeed a wretched exchange you have chosen instead of God. This Qur'an which is being recited to you is a great mercy for you; it is presenting the reality to you in diverse ways so you may understand, but instead of mercy, you are demanding punishment, and instead of bread, you are demanding stones—stating that until you are shown that punishment of which the Qur'an warns, you are not ready to believe it. At the end, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is consoled: a messenger's duty is not to bring punishment, but is only to warn and give glad tidings. Perform your duty of warning and giving glad tidings, and leave those whose hearts have been sealed to their state; your Lord is Forgiving, Merciful, due to which He is granting them respite. If He wanted to punish them immediately, He would have broken their backs; but His every work is accompanied by wisdom, so trust in Him and remain busy in your work with patience. Read the subsequent verses in this light:

'And [remember] when We said to the angels, "Prostrate to Adam," and they prostrated except Iblees. He was of the jinn and departed from [i.e., disobeyed] the command of his Lord. Then will you take him and his descendants as allies other than Me while they are enemies to you? Wretched for the wrongdoers is the exchange.'

Iblees Was of the Jinn: The story of Adam and Iblees has been discussed in detail under Surah Al-Baqarah and Al-A'raf. Here, regarding his origin, remember that he was of the jinn. He disobeyed God's command and was rejected. Iblees is no independent and immortal creature as some assume, but is the title of that jinn who deceived Adam. His hostility towards Adam and his offspring is well known. His offspring are carrying out their forefather's mission with great skill, and he has chosen such clever accomplices among humans to mislead others that even Iblees would admire their skill and praise them: 'Well done, my sons! In the art of misleading, you have surpassed even me!'

Regret Over the Quraysh's Foolishness: Recalling this primordial hostility of Iblees and his offspring, regret is expressed over the condition of the arrogant of the Quraysh—showing that it is the limit of your misfortune that you have made Iblees and his offspring your resort and protectors, leaving God. Then, turning away from them, He stated: 'Wretched for the wrongdoers is the exchange'—what a wretched exchange they have chosen instead of God!

Worship of Jinn Among the Arab Polytheists: Remember what we have written under the commentary of Surah Al-An'am—that the Arab polytheists worshipped the jinn in various forms. They believed in separate jinn and spirits for every valley and mountain, and to save themselves from their harms, they praised them and offered them gifts. Some jinn were considered so dangerous that to please them, people even sacrificed their children. They assumed that if the jinn was not pleased through the sacrifice of a child, he would destroy all their children.

The Qur'an has reminded here that Iblees was of the jinn, showing the polytheists: 'O shameless ones! Whose ancestor showed such hostility towards your ancestor, you have become his devotees and disciples?!'

The Ultimate Limit of Misfortune: 'Wretched for the wrongdoers is the exchange' refers to the polytheists. This phrase expresses astonishment and regret—showing that these wrongdoers chose the primordial enemy of their father and his offspring as an exchange for God; this is indeed the ultimate limit of misfortune.

'I did not make them witnesses to the creation of the heavens and the earth or to the creation of themselves, and I would not have taken the misleaders as assistants.'

Description of the Sarcastic Style: The style of this verse is sarcastic—showing that these devils have been associated so generously in My sovereignty and rights, whereas neither did I call them at the creation of the heavens and the earth to assist Me, nor did I invite them at their own creation to help Me, nor was I ever to choose the misleaders as My supporters; yet today, they have been associated in My sovereignty in such a way as if all these achievements have been performed by them.

'And [warn of] the Day He will say, "Call My 'partners' whom you claimed," and they will invite them, but they will not respond to them. And We will put between them [a valley of] destruction.'

'Mawbiq' means a valley of destruction, a pit of ruin.

The Day of Extreme Self-Concern: This reminds of that hour of deprivation on the Day of Resurrection, when He will tell these polytheists: 'Call those whom you claimed as My partners to help you,' and they will call them, but they will not respond to them, because they will be occupied with their own self-concern; how can they settle others' cases? He stated that a valley of destruction will be placed between them—neither can these go to them, nor can those reach them.

'And the criminals will see the Fire and will realize that they are to fall into it. And they will not find from it an escape.'

The Picture of Helplessness: The criminals will see with open eyes that Hell is ready before them, and they are surely to fall into it, but they will find no escape from it. This is a picture of their helplessness—showing that they will fall into Hell while seeing it with their own eyes.

'And We have certainly diversified in this Quran for the people from every [kind of] description, but man has ever been, most of anything, quarrelsome.'

The Meaning of 'Description' (Mathal): 'Description' here refers to those realities of the unseen world and the Hereafter which have been related above in metaphorical styles to warn people, and 'man' refers to those very quarrelsome opponents with whom the discussion is ongoing; but to express aversion, the statement is put in general terms. He stated: We have explained every kind of warning in this Qur'an in diverse ways to warn people, but man is highly quarrelsome; instead of benefiting from them, he always searches for some ground for objection.

'And nothing has prevented the people from believing when guidance came to them and from asking forgiveness of their Lord except that there should befall them the precedent of the former peoples or that the punishment should come [directly] before them.'

Demanding Heavenly Wrath Instead of the Heavenly Feast: The meaning is: the Qur'an has explained all warnings, leaving nothing hidden; but those who want to evade the reality always find some excuse.
