Now, those people demand: 'Either that destruction should fall upon us which fell upon previous nations and of which the Qur'an warns, or the punishment should appear before our eyes.' Without this, they are not ready to believe in this guidance and seek forgiveness. The Qur'an is spreading a heavenly feast before them, but they demand heavenly wrath; what can be the cure for such doomed ones? They do not know that when divine punishment appears, it does not just show itself and return, but completely destroys them; so after its appearance, what will they believe in?

'And We send not the messengers except as bringers of good tidings and warners. And those who disbelieve dispute by [using] falsehood to futilely attempt thereby [to refute] the truth and have taken My verses and what they are warned of in ridicule.'

The Purpose of Raising Messengers: This is the answer to the aforementioned demand for punishment. He stated: We do not send messengers to bring punishment, but only to warn and give glad tidings—warning the disbelievers of punishment and giving glad tidings of Paradise to the believers. Demanding them to show the punishment and Resurrection is completely absurd, equivalent to attempting to defeat the truth through falsehood. Those who commit this mischief have not understood the reality of punishment and the Resurrection; they have made Our warning verses and God's seizure a mockery.

'And who is more unjust than one who is reminded of the verses of his Lord but turns away from them and forgets what his hands have sent forth? Indeed, We have placed over their hearts coverings, lest they understand it, and in their ears deafness. And if you invite them to guidance - they will never be guided, then, ever.'

Regret Over the Condition of the Doomed: This expresses regret over the condition of these unfortunate people—showing who can be more unjust to his own self than one who is reminded through Allah's verses, yet turns away from them and demands the punishment, not realizing that due to the deeds he has committed, he deserves divine punishment at every moment. If he is receiving respite, it is purely because of God's mercy.

The Punishment of Sealing of Hearts: He stated: as a punishment for their deeds, they have become deserving of the sealing of hearts. For this reason, We have placed coverings over their hearts so they do not understand the Qur'an, and deafness in their ears so they do not hear it. The actual structure is: 'akinnatan an yafqahoohu wa fee aazaanihim waqran min an yasma'oohu.' According to the well-known rule of grammar, 'min' or 'karahatan' is omitted before 'an', and on the principle of contrast, 'an yasma'oohu' is omitted after 'fee aazaanihim waqran'. 'And if you invite them to guidance - they will never be guided, then, ever.'—obviously, when their hearts have been covered and their ears have been made deaf under God's law, no matter how much you recite Allah's verses to them, they cannot accept guidance. Leave their case to Allah, and remain busy in your work with patience until the proof is fully established against them.

'And your Lord is the Forgiving, full of mercy. If He were to impose blame upon them for what they earned, He would have hastened for them the punishment. But for them is an appointment from which they will never find a refuge.'

The Wisdom Behind Granting Respite to the Criminals: 'Mo'il' means a place of refuge. This explains: when they are such wicked people who are never going to accept guidance, what is the benefit of keeping them on the earth? Why not send upon them that punishment which they demand and put an end to them? He stated that this is because God is Forgiving and Merciful, due to which He grants respite to the criminals to the ultimate limit. Otherwise, if He wanted to punish them immediately for their deeds, He could do so immediately, and no one could hold His hand. But Allah does not make haste; rather, He has appointed a time for their seizure. When that time comes, they will find no refuge to escape.

'And those cities - We destroyed them when they wronged, and We made for their destruction an appointed time.'

The Testimony of History: This points to the testimony of history—showing that the ruins of these towns are before you, which We destroyed as a punishment for their wrong. You pass by their ruins in your journeys; obtain a lesson of warning from their end. We had appointed a time for their destruction too, and as soon as it came, they were ruined.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 60 to 82: The Doctrinal Foundations of Patience: Previously, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was exhorted to patience and steadfastness against the enemies of truth and the arrogant, and now in the subsequent verses, the completion of this theme is presented. At this point, the Surah reaches its climax. Regarding patience, remember that it is no negative thing, but is a positive reality. On it depends the preservation of all truth. One who does not possess this quality can neither pay the right of God nor of His servants. To make this quality firm, it is necessary that its doctrinal foundations be established in the heart. Without this, patience cannot be maintained. These doctrinal foundations are three:

First, whatever occurs in this world occurs under the permission, will, and command of God. Without His permission and will, not even a single particle can move from its place.

Second, God is absolute Goodness and Wise; for this reason, His every will is accompanied by goodness and wisdom. If He grants respite to the people of falsehood, it is not because He likes falsehood or is helpless before it, but because He nourishes some great goodness through it. Similarly, if He subjects the people of truth to trials and calamities, it is not because He likes their trials, but because He opens the paths of some great goodness for them in this way. Third, man's knowledge is limited; for this reason, he cannot understand the wisdom of His every will in this world. All the secrets of His will will be unveiled only in the Hereafter. The correct conduct for a man in this world is to remain patient and grateful under all God's decisions, performing his duty, and remaining satisfied that within today's bitterness lies a hidden sweetness, the cup of which will surely be presented tomorrow.

A Training Journey of Moses: To understand this secret of the universe, the story of a training journey of Moses is related here. The secrets of wisdom manifest in the events of life in a way they do not manifest through mere description. Moses was given a nation which was highly weak, and the enemy they faced was highly tyrannical; for this reason, divine wisdom required that Moses become highly firm and rooted in patience so that he could face hostile conditions with absolute determination. Consequently, God sent him to a servant of His whom He had blessed with some specific knowledge. This servant, by God's command, unveiled some secrets of His will before Moses, which were highly important for his training in patience and contentment.

Misunderstanding of the Commentators: In our view, this was the purpose of Moses's journey; but some commentators have written a baseless thing—claiming that Moses had arrogantly stated one day: 'At this time, there is no one more knowing than me,' and as a punishment, Allah sent him to a servant of His to show him that a more knowing person existed. Firstly, why would Moses speak such an improper thing? And even if he did, he spoke no wrong. It is a reality that the prophet is the most knowing person of his time, and he declares this openly before his nation. Every prophet said this to his nation: 'Indeed, I know from Allah what you do not know.' But no prophet's statement was ever taken as arrogance, nor did he deserve any punishment because of it; why then would Moses alone deserve punishment? This story of their descent is completely baseless. The actual reality is what we pointed out. Now read the verses:

'And [remember] when Moses said to his servant, "I will not cease [traveling] until I reach the junction of the two seas or continue for a long period [i.e., Huquba]."'

The Meaning of 'Fata': We have translated 'fata' as companion/disciple, which carries both the meaning of being a youth and clarifies his relationship with Moses. The words in which Moses addressed this youth show that he was not merely a servant, but was a young companion who was his disciple and also served him.

The Meaning of 'Junction of the Two Seas' (Majma'-al-Bahrayn): This probably refers to the place of connection of the Gulf of Aqaba and Suez, from where Moses crossed with the Children of Israel in the subsequent stages.

The Meaning of 'Huqub': 'Huqub' means an era, year, eighty years, or even a longer duration.

The direction for this journey was given to Moses through revelation. He expressed this to his disciple, and his every word reflects his determination, zeal, and passion for this journey. He stated: 'Either I will reach that destination which has been indicated to me, or I will spend years in search of it.' This means: if you have the determination, accompany me in this journey; otherwise, I am setting out on this favorite journey with this determination:

'Either I will reach my beloved, or I will lose my life in this path.'

'But when they reached the junction between them, they forgot their fish, and it took its course into the sea, slipping away.'

The Meaning of 'Sarab': 'Sarab' means water flowing away from a vessel.

A Strange Event: A part of the story is omitted here which is clear from the context—meaning: they both traveled and reached the junction of the two seas. Here, they sat near a rock to rest, and when they moved from there, they forgot the fish they had taken for their meal. After moving some distance, the disciple remembered the fish and returned to take it, but when he reached, he saw that the fish had slipped away into the water. This event was so strange that the disciple—probably out of fear that Moses would not believe such a strange thing and he might face his anger—did not mention it to him, and they kept moving. The disciple remained in confusion whether to mention it or not, and Moses assumed that the disciple had taken the fish.

'So when they had passed beyond, [Moses] said to his servant, "Bring us our morning meal. We have certainly suffered in this, our journey, [much] fatigue."

'Ghadaa'' means morning meal, and 'nasab' means fatigue. Using 'ghadaa'' here is a clear indicator that the fish was cooked/roasted. For a raw or alive fish, using the word 'ghadaa'' is completely inappropriate.

Ultimately, when they had moved some distance from there, Moses said to the disciple: 'This journey has fatigued us; bring the meal so we may eat.'

'He said, "Did you see when we retired to the rock? Indeed, I forgot the fish. And none made me forget it except Satan - that I should mention it. And it took its course into the sea amazingly."'

The Disciple's Apology: The disciple had to reveal the secret. Hesitating and fearing, he said: 'What can I say! When we retired to the rock, I forgot the fish, and indeed none made me forget it except Satan. Then, when I returned, I saw that the fish took its course into the sea amazingly.'

The word 'Ara'ayta' (Did you see) here expresses the speaker's hesitation, like we say: 'What can I say! Look at this.' 'And none made me forget it except Satan' are words of apology for his shortcoming.

'[Moses] said, "That is what we were seeking." So they returned, following their footprints.'

Discovering the Destination: The disciple spoke with fear, not knowing what anger he would face; but Moses was delighted to hear this news. He stated: 'This is what we were seeking!' It appears that the sign of their destination was shown to Moses through revelation: at the place where their fish would become alive and take its course into the water, they would meet that special servant of God who would reveal some secrets of His will to him. Consequently, receiving this sign, they returned immediately, tracing their footprints to the place where this event occurred.

'And they found a servant from among Our servants to whom We had given mercy from Us and had taught him from Us a [certain] knowledge.'

Meeting Al-Khidr: Here they met a special servant of God whom Allah had blessed with His favor and taught some specific knowledge. Who was he? The Qur'an has not mentioned his name, but has described his specific attributes. In some reports, his name is mentioned as Khidr. Since we have no reason to deny these reports, we adopt this name.

Al-Khidr Was a Prophet: Various indications show that Al-Khidr was a prophet. The first indication is that a great prophet and messenger like Moses was sent to him to obtain knowledge and training. If Al-Khidr had not been a prophet, sending a prophet to a non-prophet for training would have been inappropriate. Although no prophet of this name is mentioned in the Qur'an or the Torah, this is of no importance; the Qur'an itself states that there are many prophets whose stories have not been related. The same is the case with the Torah. Note that Allah has preferred some of His prophets over others in various ways. Al-Khidr had preference in a specific aspect, and similarly Moses had preference. Moses learning some things from him does not imply that Al-Khidr had absolute preference over Moses.

The second indication is that the attributes described for him by Allah are befitting only for prophets—such as: 'a servant from among Our servants', 'We had given him mercy from Us', 'We had taught him from Us a [certain] knowledge'. Furthermore, he himself stated regarding his actions: 'And I did it not of my own accord'—all these are proof that he was a prophet who received revelation, and possessed this distinction that Allah revealed some secrets of His will to him.

Commentary on Verses 66 to 68: 'Moses said to him, "May I follow you on [the condition] that you teach me from what you have been taught of sound judgment?" He said, "Indeed, with me you will never be able to have patience. And how can you have patience for that which you do not encompass in knowledge?"'

'Rushdan' means knowledge and wisdom, and here it refers to that specific knowledge regarding the secrets of the universe which Allah bestowed upon Al-Khidr. Moses requested him: 'If you teach me some of that knowledge which Allah has taught you, allow me to remain in your service for some time.' Al-Khidr replied: 'I do not refuse, but you will not be able to have patience with me. Such actions will be performed by me whose secrets and wisdom you will not know, and those actions will appear strange and improper in your eyes; how then can you have patience with them?'

'[Moses] said, "You will find me, if Allah wills, patient, and I will not disobey you in [any] order."'

Moses promised: 'If Allah wills, you will find me patient, and I will never disobey any of your orders.'

'He said, "Then if you follow me, do not ask me about anything until I make to you an announcement about it."' After this promise, Al-Khidr allowed him to accompany him on the condition that whatever he did, Moses should watch silently, asking no question about it until Al-Khidr himself initiated the discussion.

'So they set out, until when they had embarked on the ship, He [i.e., Al-Khidr] tore it open. [Moses] said, "Have you torn it open to drown its people? You have certainly done a grave thing."

'Imra' means a strange and improper thing.

The Event of the Ship: After this agreement, they both set out on their journey and embarked on a ship. After embarking, Al-Khidr pulled out a plank from the ship to make it defective. Seeing this, Moses (peace be upon him) could not remain silent, and ignoring his agreement, he exclaimed: 'Have you done this to drown the people of the ship? You have indeed done a highly improper thing!'

Commentary on Verses 72 and 73: '[Al-Khidr] said, "Did I not say that with me you would never be able to have patience?" [Moses] said, "Do not blame me for what I forgot and do not cover [i.e., burden] me in my matter with difficulty."

'Irhaq' means to burden someone beyond his power or to put him into trouble. 'Do not burden me in my matter with difficulty' means: do not treat my matter with severity.

Al-Khidr reminded Moses of his statement: 'Did I not tell you that you would never be able to have patience with me!' Moses apologized immediately: 'It was a slip; forgive me, and do not treat my matter with severity.'

Commentary on Verses 74 to 76: 'So they set out, until when they met a boy, He [i.e., Al-Khidr] killed him. [Moses] said, "Have you killed a pure soul for other than [having killed] a soul? You have certainly done a detestable thing." [Al-Khidr] said, "Did I not tell you that with me you would never be able to have patience?" [Moses] said, "If I should ask you about anything after this, then do not keep me as a companion. You have obtained from me an excuse."

The Event of Killing the Boy: They set out again. Along the way, they met a boy, and Al-Khidr killed him. Seeing this, Moses became highly angry, saying: 'What have you done! You have killed an innocent soul without him having killed anyone! You have indeed done a highly abominable thing!' Al-Khidr reminded him again: 'Did I not tell you that you would never be able to have patience with me!' Moses apologized again and said: 'If I ask you anything after this, you have the right not to keep me as a companion. Then, you will be absolved of any blame regarding me.'

Commentary on Verses 77 and 78: 'So they set out, until when they came to the people of a town, they asked its people for food, but they refused to offer them hospitality. And they found therein a wall about to collapse, so He [i.e., Al-Khidr] restored it. [Moses] said, "If you wished, you could have taken for it a payment." [Al-Khidr] said, "This is parting between me and you. I will inform you of the interpretation of that about which you were unable to have patience."

The Event of the Town: They set out again until they reached a town. They were hungry, so they asked the people of the town for food. Noble people consider welcoming travelers as a privilege even without being asked, but the people of this town were so miserly that they refused to offer food to these two noble travelers. Despite their miserliness, Al-Khidr rebuilt a wall in their town which was about to collapse. Moses could not remain silent at this, and objected: 'Why did you perform this hard labor for these unworthy and miserly people without any payment? You could have at least charged some wage for this labor!' Al-Khidr replied: 'Now, the limit has been reached. This is parting between me and you. Come, I will tell you the reality of those matters upon which you could not have patience.' The context indicates that Al-Khidr declared this parting not only because Moses was silenced by his own promise, but because the teaching of wisdom which he wanted to give to Moses had been completed.

The Wisdom of the Events: 'As for the ship, it belonged to poor people working at sea. So I intended to cause defect in it as there was after them a king who seized every [good] ship by force.'

Now Al-Khidr explained to Moses the wisdom of each of those actions which Allah had commanded him to perform. The wisdom behind pulling out a plank of the ship was: this ship belonged to poor people who earned their living through it. Ahead was a king who was seizing all good ships by force, probably for some military purpose. Al-Khidr stated: 'I intended to make this ship defective so that the king's men would consider it useless and not seize it, and these poor people would be saved from losing their sole source of livelihood.'

This is an example showing that if the poor, needy, and righteous suffer any financial or economic loss in the world, some major benefit of theirs is hidden within this loss; so they must remain patient, content with God's decision, and believe that His every decision is accompanied by wisdom, though no one can encompass His wisdom.

Commentary on Verses 80 and 81: 'And as for the boy, his parents were believers, and we feared that he would overburden them by transgression and disbelief. So we intended that their Lord should substitute for them one better than him in purity and nearer to mercy.'

'Ruhm' means compassion, sympathy, affection, and love. 'One better than him in purity and nearer to mercy' means: one who is pure in character and more compassionate and obedient towards his parents.

The Wisdom Behind Killing the Boy: This explains the wisdom behind killing the boy: his parents were believers, and he was to grow up as a disbeliever and rebel; we feared that when he grew up, he would trouble his parents with his rebellion and ingratitude. We intended that instead of this rebellious son, Allah grant them offspring who would be pure and compassionate. This is an example showing that if believers suffer any loss of life, some great goodness is hidden within it which Allah alone knows; so they must remain patient. When its wisdom is revealed, it will become clear that whatever God did was for their benefit.

'And as for the wall, it belonged to two orphan boys in the city, and there was beneath it a treasure for them, and their father had been righteous. So your Lord intended that they reach maturity and extract their treasure, as a mercy from your Lord. And I did it not of my own accord. That is the interpretation of that about which you were unable to have patience.'

The Wisdom Behind Rebuilding the Wall: This explains why he rebuilt the wall of those miserly people without any payment: this wall belonged to two orphan boys, and beneath it, their father—who was a righteous man—had hidden a treasure for them so it might serve them after him. If this wall had collapsed, the treasure would have fallen into the hands of the miserly people of the town, and these orphans would have been deprived of it. For this reason, your Lord intended that this wall remain standing until these children reached maturity, so they could extract their treasure themselves; this was a mercy from your Lord upon these orphans, not upon the miserly people of the town. 'And I did it not of my own accord'—at the end, Al-Khidr also clarified that he did none of these actions of his own accord, but performed each by God's command.

This is an example showing that the respite the wicked receive in the world is not because He wants to nourish the wicked, but because some great goodness is being nourished under their cover, though we do not know it. Through this respite, the wicked merely establish the proof against themselves. On the other hand, through their hands, Allah refines the people of truth, and they are the actual purpose of this world's creation.

Some Related Benefits of Verses 60 to 82: The primary wisdom of Moses's story has been pointed out above, but some related benefits are also worthy of attention. We point to them in brief:

Wisdom is Bestowed Only upon the Sincere Seeker: First, what is called wisdom is not received by every claimant, but is bestowed only upon the sincere seeker. It is not something to be bought with money, but requires great struggle. It is not obtained while sitting at home, but sometimes requires traveling over land and sea in search of a guide, and there is a possibility of spending one's entire life in this journey. This is the path of love where one must go with complete humility, sacrificing one's pride. Unless a person is ready to make this sacrifice, he should not step into this path. This is a treasury of great goodness which Allah has reserved only for those who sacrifice every desire for the sake of wisdom. In the story of Moses and Khidr, all these matters are clear on their own.

Second, the shariah is our actual guide; our duty is to follow it under all circumstances, and if we see anyone violating it, we must object—even if the action was committed by a guide like Al-Khidr. See: Moses, despite being sent to Al-Khidr by Allah, did not show patience against any action which he found contrary to the shariah. Moses became satisfied only when Al-Khidr assured him that he performed all these actions by God's command, doing nothing of his own accord; and Moses's satisfaction was not based merely on Al-Khidr's statement, but he knew beforehand through revelation that Al-Khidr was a special servant of God who performed actions only by His command.

The Concept of 'Aqt_aab' and 'Abdaal' is Baseless: For this reason, we consider the view of those completely baseless who claim: 'In every era, there are some saints (aqt_aab and abdaal) like Al-Khidr who are their own proof of truth, and their actions are not to be judged on the scale of the shariah because they perform actions directly under God's command.' In our view, these terms (aqt_aab and abdaal) are completely baseless, holding no proof in the Qur'an or Hadith. But even if anyone is assumed to be such a saint, how can we tolerate his actions if they are contrary to the shariah? Moses tolerated Al-Khidr's actions because he knew through revelation that Al-Khidr performed actions by God's command; but what means do we have to know about anyone that he is such a saint and has been given the authority to violate the shariah? The sole means of knowing God's pleasure and commands is the Book of Allah and the Sunnah of the Prophet. For this reason, let alone any saint, even if Al-Khidr himself were to appear today and kill anyone, offering the defense that he did so by God's command, we would reject his defense and sentence him to death under the law 'a life for a soul' present in the Qur'an, because we have no proof from God that his act of killing is permitted.

Removal of Some Doubts: Third, there is a grammatical variation in the verse which might cause a doubt—showing that on one hand, Al-Khidr attributes all these actions to God: 'And I did it not of my own accord'; on the other hand, regarding the ship, he stated: 'So I intended to cause defect in it'; regarding the boy, he stated: 'and we feared'; and regarding the wall, he stated: 'So your Lord intended'. A question arises: why did he use different styles for the same matter? When he did everything by God's command, the clear style was: 'your Lord intended'; why then did he say: 'I intended to cause defect'? Furthermore, when he did everything alone, why did he use the plural 'we feared' and 'we intended'? If it is assumed that he included Allah in it, using 'we feared' for Allah is completely inappropriate, because how can God fear anything? To remove these doubts, keep in view the following points:

First, when a servant's will is exactly the same as God's will, he can attribute it to God as well as to himself. The variation is only for the sake of eloquence. For example, sometimes respect and etiquette require that an action be attributed to the servant himself rather than to God. Here, pulling out a plank of the ship was outwardly a defective act, so Al-Khidr attributed it to himself. On the other hand, safeguarding the orphans' treasure was outwardly a good act, so he attributed it directly to Allah.

Second, in such contexts, when a speaker uses the plural pronoun for himself, he speaks as the representative of the entire group of forces under whose direction and support he performs the action. Since Al-Khidr performed all these actions as an agent of the divine decree, he could use the plural pronoun. The benefit of this was that the action did not remain his individual action, but became the action of the agents of the divine decree.

Third, 'we feared' (khasheena) used here does not relate to Allah, but was used by Al-Khidr to express his own judgment. The command he received through revelation was: 'Kill this boy, because his parents are believers, and he will grow up as a disbeliever.' From this command, Al-Khidr inferred that the boy would trouble his parents, which was the reason why his execution was commanded. This was his own inference, and was not explicitly stated in the divine command. For this reason, Al-Khidr mentioned it as his own fear and used the word 'we feared'.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 83 to 98: A Question Instigated by the Jews: In the following, the reply to a question regarding Dhul-Qarnayn is presented. This question was raised by the Jews, and their purpose was the same as that of the question regarding the Companions of the Cave—to trouble the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and put obstacles in the way of the call; but they made the Quraysh the medium to present it before the Prophet. That this question was instigated by the Jews is highly probable because they had a specific interest in Dhul-Qarnayn. They considered him their benefactor, and he was mentioned in the scriptures of their prophets. The Qur'an answered it—knowing the mischievous mindset of the objectors—because the life of Dhul-Qarnayn could be highly instructive for the arrogant and wealthy whose mindset is under discussion throughout this Surah.

Dhul-Qarnayn: Who was Dhul-Qarnayn? Our commentators hold different views on this. Generally, people have considered Alexander of Macedon as Dhul-Qarnayn. Some consider the Persian king Cyrus (known as Khosrow or Cyrus the Great) or Darius as the one meant. Another view is that it refers to a king of Yemen. Among these, the last view has no reliable proof. The attributes of Dhul-Qarnayn described by the Qur'an also do not apply to Alexander. The Qur'an has described Dhul-Qarnayn as a pure monotheist, a true believer, and a highly just and compassionate ruler, whereas Alexander possessed none of these attributes. Alexander's conquests were also not as vast as those described by the Qur'an for Dhul-Qarnayn. There is also no evidence of the title Dhul-Qarnayn being used for him. Some eastern and western campaigns of Darius are indeed found in history, but he was not the pioneer in this path, but was the one who stabilized the empire established by Cyrus.

On the other hand, Cyrus possessed several such attributes because of which he can be considered the actual person meant by Dhul-Qarnayn. He was a believing and just king whose noble character and popularity are accepted by all ancient and modern historians. The Qur'an has mentioned three of his campaigns, two of which (eastern and western) are proven historically. Regarding the third campaign, though historians do not speak with certainty, its indications exist.

Mention of Dhul-Qarnayn in the Scriptures of the Jews: This king has a great favor upon the Jews—showing that he saved them from the captivity of Babylon, and through his help, Jerusalem and the Temple were rebuilt. The prophets of the Jews foretold his advent. In Isaiah: 'Thus says the Lord to His anointed, to Cyrus, whose right hand I have held, to subdue nations before him; and I will loose the loins of kings...' (45:1).

In this prophecy, 'Cyrus' is the Greek pronunciation of his name. Similarly, a vision of Daniel is related: 'Then I lifted up my eyes, and saw, and, behold, there stood before the river a ram which had two horns: and the two horns were high; but one was higher than the other, and the higher came up last. I saw the ram pushing westward, and northward, and southward; so that no beasts might stand before him, neither was there any that could deliver out of his hand...' (Daniel 8:3-4).

The interpretation of this vision was given to Daniel by Gabriel—showing that the two horns refer to the two kingdoms of Media and Persia which the promised king would bring under his sway. Consequently, it is a reality that Cyrus brought both these kingdoms under his sway, and on this basis, his title became Dhul-Qarnayn (the two-horned one). A statue of his was discovered near Pasargadae in recent times, erected in the era of Artaxerxes I. In it, two horns are seen rising from his crown; this might be merely as a symbol of majesty, or might contain the same secret referenced above. Read the verses in this light: 'And they ask you, [O Muhammad], about Dhul-Qarnayn. Say, "I will recite to you about him a remembrance."

The Title Dhul-Qarnayn for Cyrus: The title Dhul-Qarnayn for Cyrus (Khosrow) was adopted by the Jews of Arabia. 'I will recite' (sa-atloo) contains a appeal—showing that if you ask, I will relate a part of his story which is instructive. I hope you will hear it and benefit from it. The word 'remembrance' (zikr) points to the warning and instruction implicit in it.

'Indeed, We established him upon the earth and We gave him from everything a way [i.e., means].'

The Meaning of 'Sabab': 'Sabab' basically means a way and medium. Here, 'We gave him from everything a means' means that in his kingdom, all kinds of means and resources were present.

The Great Empire of Cyrus: Regarding Cyrus, who was originally known as Kurush, remember that in the middle of the sixth century BC, he was appointed ruler of his father Cambyses's small state of Anshan. As soon as he came to power, he had to face the invasion of the king of Media, whom he defeated. In the subsequent few years, he subdued all the major states of his time, and his empire extended from Balkh and Makran to the Mediterranean Sea. Before him, no such vast and grand empire had been established by anyone.

'So he followed a way.'

'Ittiba'' means to pursue and follow. 'So he followed a way' (fa-atba'a sababa) means that he evaluated the means and resources, arranging them. Then, in a wider sense, it became used for preparing for a campaign.

حَتَّى إِذَا بَلَغَ مَغْرِبَ الشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَغْرُبُ فِي عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ وَوَجَدَ عِنْدَهَا قَوْمًا قُلْنَا يَا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّا أَنْ تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّا أَنْ تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْنًا۔ 

**Concept of "Maghrib ash-Shams" (The Setting Place of the Sun):** Reaching the setting place of the sun is an expression indicating that he reached the sea coast in the West. "He found it setting in a murky spring (عَيْنٍ حَمِئَةٍ)" means that at the time of sunset, it appeared as if the sun was sinking into a dark spring. This phrase aims to express the fact that Dhul-Qarnayn brought under his rule the entire known world of the West, as far as human footsteps of that era could reach. Beyond that lay the ocean, from which it appeared that this was the final boundary of the world in the West and that the sun set there.

**Cyrus's Western Campaign:** This refers to the first campaign of Cyrus (Kai Khosrow), which started from his capital Ecbatana (modern Hamadan) towards the West. In this campaign, he conquered Media (modern Iraq and Syria) and Lydia (modern Turkey). In the Lydian capital, Sardis (near Smyrna), he defeated its ruler, Croesus, who had the support of the empires of Babylon, Egypt, and Sparta. In this campaign, Cyrus's march stopped only at the shores of the Mediterranean Sea.

وَوَجَدَ عِنْدَهَا قَوْمًا قُلْنَا يَا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ إِمَّا أَنْ تُعَذِّبَ وَإِمَّا أَنْ تَتَّخِذَ فِيهِمْ حُسْنًا۔ 

The word "Qawl" (saying/statement) is also used to describe a situation, authority, and attitude. The meaning is that, as a result of this victory, the subjects who came under his control were placed at his feet in such a manner, and he was granted such authority over them, that whether he punished them or showed them kindness, there was no one to interfere with his authority and power. This does not mean that God verbally permitted him to commit injustice or practice justice as if he had an equal right to both. The right belongs only to justice and kindness. Every king and ruler is commanded by God to maintain justice and kindness, but they are also given the leeway to choose injustice and unfairness [as free will]. If he practices justice, he will receive his reward from God, and if he commits injustice, he will suffer its punishment. The ultimate guidance for rulers is the one given by Allah Almighty when addressing Prophet David: "O David, indeed We have made you a successor upon the earth, so judge between the people in truth and do not follow [your] desire, as it will lead you astray from the way of Allah. Indeed, those who go astray from the way of Allah will have a severe punishment for having forgotten the Day of Account." (Sad: 26)

In the verse under discussion, Dhul-Qarnayn is addressed in a similar manner, just as Prophet Solomon (peace be upon him) is addressed in Surah Sad: "This is Our gift, so grant or withhold without account." (Sad: 39)

Evidently, this is merely an expression of the freedom granted to human beings, whether to choose the path of disbelief or belief. Similarly, after receiving Allah's blessings, humans have the option to choose the path of generosity, benevolence, and spending, or that of misery and stinginess. They are given freedom in both, but only one is pleasing in the sight of God, and that is what He has demanded of His servants.

Tafseer of Verses 87 to 88: قَالَ أَمَّا مَنْ ظَلَمَ فَسَوْفَ نُعَذِّبُهُ ثُمَّ يُرَدُّ إِلَى رَبِّهِ فَيُعَذِّبُهُ عَذَابًا نُكْرًا (٨٧) وَأَمَّا مَنْ آمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَلَهُ جَزَاءً الْحُسْنَى وَسَنَقُولُ لَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِنَا يُسْرًا (٨٨) ۔ 

**Statement through Action (Qawl bi-Lisan al-'Amal):** Just as the statement above represents a state of affairs (bi-lisan al-haal), this statement represents action. That is, he testified through his attitude and conduct that those who choose the path of injustice and corruption [will be punished], while those who believe and do good deeds will be honored with a good end by God, and we too will adopt a very lenient policy regarding their affairs.

Expressing attitude and conduct through "Qawl" (speech/saying) is a well-known style in the Arabic language. The statement of the Jews quoted in the Qur'an: "They said, 'We hear and we disobey'" (as we clarified under Al-Baqarah: 93) is an expression of their actions and attitude. In these very verses, the word "Qawl" in the phrase "وَسَنَقُولُ لَهُ مِنْ أَمْرِنَا يُسْرًا" (And we will speak to him of our command with ease) also refers to attitude. This is because its apparent meaning is that we will adopt a gentle attitude or soft policy towards them. Based solely on the "Qulna" (We said) above and this "Qala" (He said), some people have accepted Dhul-Qarnayn as a prophet. However, in our view, this argument alone is not sufficient to consider him a prophet. Nonetheless, the evidence of his being a just and God-fearing king is found both in these verses and in history.

**Historical Evidence Regarding Dhul-Qarnayn:** Historians write that whichever city Cyrus's army conquered, they did not harm its citizens in the least. The king was a figure of mercy and compassion for the conquered nations. He completely abolished all heavy taxes and tributes. Even when his bitterest enemies were brought before him as captives, he announced their pardon. Deeply moved by his benevolent treatment, they assured the king of their unconditional loyalty. After defeating the invading king of Media, Cyrus kept him in his palace until his death, granting him ten thousand infantry and five thousand cavalry, thus maintaining his royal status.

**Dhul-Qarnayn was a Follower of Zoroastrianism:** These verses also indicate that Dhul-Qarnayn was a believer, monotheist, and a king who believed in the Hereafter. Historical accounts also support and confirm this. Cyrus was a contemporary and follower of Zoroaster. In the original teachings of Zoroaster, one finds a correct concept of belief in God and the Hereafter. Although later, like other religions, this faith was not preserved from alterations and dualistic concepts came to dominate it. Darius, in his inscriptions, thanks Ahura Mazda (God), attributes his kingdom to His grace and mercy, and prays for the guidance to remain on the right path. Obviously, this religiosity was inherited from Dhul-Qarnayn. Dhul-Qarnayn also had great reverence for the Prophets of the Children of Israel. This also contributed to strengthening his religious inclinations.

Tafseer of Verses 89 to 90: ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٨٩) حَتَّى إِذَا بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ الشَّمْسِ وَجَدَهَا تَطْلُعُ عَلَى قَوْمٍ لَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُمْ مِنْ دُونِهَا سِتْرًا (٩٠)

**Cyrus's Eastern Campaign:** This describes the second campaign of Cyrus. The words "reached the rising place of the sun" (بَلَغَ مَطْلِعَ الشَّمْسِ) make it clear that this campaign was in the easterly direction, and in this direction too, he reached the outermost limits. Historians write that this was caused by the rebellion of the wild and nomadic tribes of Makran, Kandahar, and Balkh, who had caused unrest on the eastern border of Persia. Ultimately, Dhul-Qarnayn had to rise to suppress them, and he conquered all these regions. What the Qur'an has said about these tribes—that We had provided no shelter for them against the sun—is a depiction of their extremely wild and uncivilized state. It appears that these tribes were completely unfamiliar with houses, architecture, and civilization, living in the very early stages of their wild state as nomads.

كَذَلِكَ وَقَدْ أَحَطْنَا بِمَا لَدَيْهِ خُبْرًا۔ 

**Dhul-Qarnayn Was Endowed with High Qualities:** This verse is in the exact context and position as the verse of Surah Al-Anbiya: "And We had certainly given Abraham his sound judgment before, and We were of him well-Knowing." There, pointing to the wisdom of honoring Prophet Abraham with the status of leadership and guidance, Allah states that We were well aware of him. That is, if We bestowed this honor upon him, it was not without reason, but rather We were well aware of those capabilities of his which proved him worthy of this position. Similarly, here, referring to those conquests of Dhul-Qarnayn achieved through Allah's blessings, it is stated that the capacity and capability required to manage this vast power and great trust were present within him to perfection, and We were well aware of them.

Tafseer of Verses 92 to 98: ثُمَّ أَتْبَعَ سَبَبًا (٩٢) حَتَّى إِذَا بَلَغَ بَيْنَ السَّدَّيْنِ وَجَدَ مِنْ دُونِهِمَا قَوْمًا لا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلا (٩٣) قَالُوا يَا ذَا الْقَرْنَيْنِ إِنَّ يَأْجُوجَ وَمَأْجُوجَ مُفْسِدُونَ فِي الأرْضِ فَهَلْ نَجْعَلُ لَكَ خَرْجًا عَلَى أَنْ تَجْعَلَ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَهُمْ سَدًّا (٩٤) قَالَ مَا مَكَّنِّي فِيهِ رَبِّي خَيْرٌ فَأَعِينُونِي بِقُوَّةٍ أَجْعَلْ بَيْنَكُمْ وَبَيْنَهُمْ رَدْمًا (٩٥) آتُونِي زُبَرَ الْحَدِيدِ حَتَّى إِذَا سَاوَى بَيْنَ الصَّدَفَيْنِ قَالَ انْفُخُوا حَتَّى إِذَا جَعَلَهُ نَارًا قَالَ آتُونِي أُفْرِغْ عَلَيْهِ قِطْرًا (٩٦) فَمَا اسْطَاعُوا أَنْ يَظْهَرُوهُ وَمَا اسْتَطَاعُوا لَهُ نَقْبًا (٩٧) قَالَ هَذَا رَحْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّي فَإِذَا جَاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ وَكَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي حَقًّا (٩٨) ۔ 

**Cyrus's Third Campaign and the Construction of the Barrier (Sadd):** This refers to the third campaign of Cyrus. Historians do not make much mention of this campaign, but they do state that after the conquest of Babylon, he set out on a journey toward the northeast. His destination was likely toward Turkestan to the east of the Caspian Sea. It was during this journey that he fell into a ravine and died. It appears that during this campaign, the event of building this barrier, as mentioned in the Qur'an, took place.

**Gog and Magog (Yajuj and Majuj):** This barrier, as is clear from the Qur'an, was built to prevent the attacks of Gog and Magog. By Gog and Magog is meant the descendants of Noah's son Japheth, who settled in the northern regions of Asia. Ezekiel states: "And the word of the Lord came unto me, saying, Son of man, set thy face against Gog, the land of Magog, the chief prince of Meshech and Tubal, and prophesy against him" (Ezekiel 38:1-2). "And say, Thus saith the Lord God; Behold, I am against thee, O Gog, the chief prince of Meshech and Tubal: And I will turn thee back, and leave but the sixth part of thee, and will cause thee to come up from the north parts, and will bring thee upon the mountains of Israel" (Ezekiel 39:1-2).

The names Rosh (or chief/Russia), Meshech, and Tubal still exist today in the form of Russia, Moscow, and Tobolsk, and these areas are in the far northern regions from Palestine. The tribes of Gog and Magog were settled north of the Caspian Sea and in the region of Mongolia in Central Asia. Their incursions into Iran occurred both through Turkestan and through the Caucasus Pass. The Caucasus Mountains lie directly north of Cyrus's capital. Therefore, it is highly possible that the king wished to block this pass to prevent this threat.

**The Iron Wall of the Daryal Gorge:** An iron wall exists in the Daryal Gorge of the Caucasus Mountains. Travelers have also mentioned it in their travelogues. It is narrated that the Abbasid Caliph Al-Wathiq appointed a team of fifty people to investigate this wall, and they traced its location. People attribute this wall to Darius or Anushirvan, but more evidence supports the view that Cyrus built it. For instance, it has been established through research that the northern limit of Cyrus's empire reached the Caucasus Mountains. Acquiring such a vast territory under his rule was only possible if he had taken steps to conquer this region. A city and a river by the name of Kur (Cyrus) still exist in the Caucasus region. The iron wall is referred to as "Gora", which seems to be a corrupted form of Kur. This wall is made of metal between two mountains, and a space is left at its lower part for rainwater to flow out. "They could hardly understand speech" (لا يَكَادُونَ يَفْقَهُونَ قَوْلا) indicates that these people had no interaction with other nations. Living a confined life within their territory, entirely isolated from others, they could barely understand others' language.

**Dhul-Qarnayn's Spirit of Public Service:** "مَا مَكَّنِّي فِيهِ رَبِّي خَيْرٌ فَأَعِينُونِي بِقُوَّةٍ" — Here, "quwwah" (strength) refers to labor. This means that Dhul-Qarnayn, out of generosity, did not accept the offer of the subjects to provide funds for building the wall. He said that as far as wealth is concerned, whatever my Lord has placed at my disposal is better. Implicit in the word "better" is that it is sufficient, and also that this wealth is entirely free from any taint of injustice, oppression, or plunder. However, you provide the labor, and I will erect a barrier between you and the corrupters (Gog and Magog). "Bring me sheets of iron—until, when he had filled the gap between the two mountain cliffs..." (the verse). "Zubar" is the plural of "zubrah", meaning pieces of iron. "Sadaf" means shell or void. Its dual use here (Sadafayn) takes into account both sides. Just as "Mashriqayn" (two Easts) and "Maghribayn" (two Wests) account for both sides, "Sadafayn" accounts for both edges of the gap. The purpose is to show that the space between the two mountains was completely filled.

"هَذَا رَحْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّي فَإِذَا جَاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ وَكَانَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي حَقًّا"

"Dakka'a" originally refers to a she-camel without a hump. Here it means to level or make flat. The meaning is that when such a magnificent iron and lead-lined wall was erected, such that it was neither possible for Gog and Magog to climb over it nor to tunnel through it, Dhul-Qarnayn did not, like narrow-minded people, say in a state of pride, "I have performed a feat that will never decline." Instead, with complete humility and submissiveness, he said: "This service that has been accomplished is merely a manifestation of my Lord's grace and mercy. Today this wall is invincible, but when the time of the fulfillment of my Lord's promise comes, He will level it to dust, and the promise of my Lord is bound to happen."

In the previous parable of the two men, you have read the mindset of the arrogant people of the world: when one enters his lush green garden in a state of pride, he says, "I do not think this will ever perish." After that, the mindset of a grateful servant is highlighted, who is thankful to his Lord even for his greatest achievements and remembers Allah's inevitable promise of the Hereafter.

Subsequent Subject Matter. Verses 99 to 110: Now ahead are the concluding verses of the Surah. This conclusion begins with an eloquent transition. What Dhul-Qarnayn said upon looking at his great barrier—that when the time of the manifestation of my Lord's inevitable promise of Resurrection comes, all these heights will be flattened and all these structures demolished—has been used by Allah Almighty as a means to remind about the promise of Resurrection and to warn those lost in worldly life. Thus, the theme of warning (Inzar) with which the Surah began reappears in a new style. We have pointed out in various places this characteristic of the Qur'an's coherence: surahs generally conclude with the same theme with which they begin. Take a look at the introduction and conclusion of this Surah; this feature is present here too. Recite the following verses in this light.

وَتَرَكْنَا بَعْضَهُمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَمُوجُ فِي بَعْضٍ وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَجَمَعْنَاهُمْ جَمْعًا۔ 

**Indication of Some Signs of Resurrection:** By linking to Dhul-Qarnayn's aforementioned statement: "فَإِذَا جَاءَ وَعْدُ رَبِّي جَعَلَهُ دَكَّاءَ...", Allah Almighty clarified further signs of the occurrence of the Resurrection, thereby completing Dhul-Qarnayn's statement. It is known from this verse that the boundaries of rivers and mountains will all vanish one day, and nations will crash into each other just as waves crash in the ocean. Scientific advancements have already removed the barriers of rivers and mountains anyway, but it appears that at the time of the Resurrection, even the remaining barriers will become meaningless. From this verse and the verse of Surah Al-Anbiya: "Until, when Gog and Magog are let loose, and they swiftly swarm from every mound," there is also an indication that near the Resurrection, the descendants of Japheth will spread over the entire world like a deluge, and then from within this very storm, the Resurrection will emerge. Although these matters are of an allegorical nature (Mutashabihat) and their true knowledge belongs only to the Knower of the Unseen, we cannot shut our eyes to the signs appearing in the world. The emphasis of "Jam'an" (completely/altogether) in "Fajama'nahum Jam'an" highlights the fact that the Trumpet of Resurrection will gather everyone. Young and old, the worshipper and the worshipped, the present and the absent—none will be left out. All will be brought forth.

وَعَرَضْنَا جَهَنَّمَ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلْكَافِرِينَ عَرْضًا۔

**Observation of Hell:** That is, today Hell is not visible to those who are blind of intellect and heart, but on that day We will present this Hell right before their eyes, saying: "Behold, what you could not see with the eyes of intellect, now observe it with the eyes of experience."

الَّذِينَ كَانَتْ أَعْيُنُهُمْ فِي غِطَاءٍ عَنْ ذِكْرِي وَكَانُوا لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ سَمْعًا۔

**Observation of Hell (State of the Disbelievers):** This describes the characteristic of those disbelievers, indicating that their intellect was so impaired that no reminder from the Prophet succeeded in opening their eyes, and when Our warnings were recited to them, they could not bear to hear them.

أَفَحَسِبَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَنْ يَتَّخِذُوا عِبَادِي مِنْ دُونِي أَوْلِيَاءَ إِنَّا أَعْتَدْنَا جَهَنَّمَ لِلْكَافِرِينَ نُزُلا۔

**Refutation of Partners (Shuraka):** This warns the addressees: Have these unfortunate people assumed that they can take some of Our servants as protectors and helpers besides Us, who would intercede on their behalf and save them from Us on the Day of Resurrection? This is merely their wishful thinking. For all such disbelievers, We have prepared Hell as an entertainment (hospitality), from which no one's effort or intercession will be able to rescue them.

Tafseer of Verses 103 to 106: قُلْ هَلْ نُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِالأخْسَرِينَ أَعْمَالا (١٠٣) الَّذِينَ ضَلَّ سَعْيُهُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا وَهُمْ يَحْسَبُونَ أَنَّهُمْ يُحْسِنُونَ صُنْعًا (١٠٤) أُولَئِكَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَلِقَائِهِ فَحَبِطَتْ أَعْمَالُهُمْ فَلا نُقِيمُ لَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ وَزْنًا (١٠٥) ذَلِكَ جَزَاؤُهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ بِمَا كَفَرُوا وَاتَّخَذُوا آيَاتِي وَرُسُلِي هُزُوًا (١٠٦)

**A Blow to the Vanity of the Arrogant:** This strikes at the core vanity of those who are arrogant in worldly life. They believe they are playing a very successful game and doing great deeds, but tell them that in the Hereafter, the most unsuccessful and losing ones will be those whose entire struggle is in pursuit of this world. It is stated that their love for this world made them entirely heedless and fearless of Our verses and Our meeting, so all their deeds will become completely weightless and meaningless. Today they think of themselves as highly significant, but on the Day of Resurrection, We will assign no weight to them. In their pride, they denied Our signs and mocked Our messengers; now, in retribution for this, let them taste the punishment of Hell.

Tafseer of Verses 107 to 108: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ كَانَتْ لَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ الْفِرْدَوْسِ نُزُلا (١٠٧) خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا لا يَبْغُونَ عَنْهَا حِوَلا (١٠٨) ، 

**The Reward of the Believers:** This states the reward for choosing the path of faith and righteous deeds: that the hospitality of Paradise (Al-Firdaws) is prepared for them. They will abide therein forever, and despite staying there eternally, they will never grow weary of it. In it, their ranks will continuously rise, and the blessings will keep changing according to their desires. Consequently, they will never desire to leave it and go elsewhere.

قُلْ لَوْ كَانَ الْبَحْرُ مِدَادًا لِكَلِمَاتِ رَبِّي لَنَفِدَ الْبَحْرُ قَبْلَ أَنْ تَنْفَدَ كَلِمَاتُ رَبِّي وَلَوْ جِئْنَا بِمِثْلِهِ مَدَدًا 

**Answer to Those Demanding Miracles:** By "Kalimat" (Words) is meant those signs of Allah's power and wisdom that are spread throughout the horizons and within human souls. This is an answer to those arrogant individuals who mocked the verses of the Qur'an and demanded a visible, physical miracle from the Prophet. It is stated: Tell them that if you are seekers of signs, this universe is so full of signs that even if the ocean were to become ink, its ink would be insufficient to record them, even if another similar ocean were added to it. In Surah Luqman, this theme is expressed in even more powerful words, and it is entirely a statement of reality. There is not the slightest exaggeration in it. If the ocean itself wanted to record its own signs and wonders, all its ink would not suffice even for that. However, these signs are visible only to those who have eyes.

قُلْ إِنَّمَا أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحَى إِلَيَّ أَنَّمَا إِلَهُكُمْ إِلَهٌ وَاحِدٌ فَمَنْ كَانَ يَرْجُو لِقَاءَ رَبِّهِ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلا صَالِحًا وَلا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبَادَةِ رَبِّهِ أَحَدًا۔

That is, tell those who demand signs: "I am only a human being like you. Showing signs is the work of God. I have announced my messengerhood, so I convey to you the revelation that comes to me. I have not claimed divinity such that I can show a miracle according to your demand. The revelation that comes to me is that your deity is only One Deity. So whoever expects the meeting with his Lord, let him worship Him without associating anyone with Him."

These are the final lines committed to paper in the commentary of this Surah. May Allah Almighty forgive any errors and create space in hearts for correct matters. And our final call is that all praise belongs to Allah, Lord of the Worlds.
